Channel; a watercourse, trench, or passage for flowing water.
Plural form of 'channel'. See 'channel'.
Chant; a song or utterance sung in measured tones.
As a verb, to sing or recite in such a manner.
Chapel; a sacred or royal place of worship, or a shrine.
Chapiter; the capital or upper ornament of a pillar or column.
Plural form of 'chapiter'. See 'chapiter'.
Chapmen; traders or merchants.
Chapt; cracked, split, or roughened by dryness.
Charashim; the valley or place of craftsmen, associated with artisan settlements in Old Testament geography.
Charge; a command, duty, burden, cost, accusation, or the act of entrusting or commanding.
Chargeable; imputable, accountable, or burdensome at another's cost.
Charged; commanded, entrusted, accused, or burdened with cost or duty.
Chargedst; means thou didst charge, command, entrust, or accuse.
A large dish, platter, or serving tray.
Charges; commands, duties, accusations, or costs.
Chargest; means commandest, entrustest, or accusest.
Charging; commanding, entrusting, accusing, or laying a burden upon.
A chariot is a wheeled war or state carriage, commonly drawn by horses.
It often signifies military strength, royal state, or swift movement.
Chariots; wheeled war or state vehicles drawn by horses.
In a charitab manner or degree.
Christian love: a gracious, self-giving love toward God and man.
Not chiefly almsgiving, but the inward love from which merciful acts may spring.
Charmed; past tense or past participial form of 'charm'. See 'charm'.
Charmer; one who uses charms or enchantments.
Plural form of 'charmer'. See 'charmer'.
Charming; present participial form of 'charm', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'charm'.
Charran; the Greek spelling of Haran, where Abraham sojourned before entering Canaan.
Chase; to pursue or drive before one.
Chased; past tense or past participial form of 'chase'. See 'chase'.
Chaseth; older third-person singular form of 'chase'. See 'chase'.
Chasing; present participial form of 'chase', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'chase'.
Chaste; pure, modest, and free from defilement.
To discipline, correct, or train by rebuke or suffering.
Not merely to punish, but often to amend.
Chastenest; older second-person singular form of 'chasten', used with 'thou'. See 'chasten'.
Chastening; present participial form of 'chasten', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'chasten'.
Chastise; to correct, punish, or discipline.
Chastised; past tense or past participial form of 'chastise'. See 'chastise'.
corrective punishment or discipline.
Chastiseth; older third-person singular form of 'chastise'. See 'chastise'.
Chatter; to make a broken, twittering, or feeble sound.
Chebar; the river or canal by which Ezekiel saw visions among the exiles.
Chebar.note: Location: Commonly identified with Shatt en Nil.

definitions: Check; to restrain, rebuke, or stop.
Checker; a variegated or checkered pattern, or one who checks or inspects.
Chedorlaomer; the eastern king who led the confederacy defeated by Abram.
Cheek; the side of the face below the eye.
Plural form of 'cheek'. See 'cheek'.
Cheer; comfort, courage, or gladness of heart.
Cheereth; older third-person singular form of 'cheer'. See 'cheer'.
Cheerful; glad, willing, or full of ready joy.
In a cheerful manner or degree.
The state, quality, or condition of being cheerfulnes.
Cheese is curdled and prepared milk used for food.
Plural form of 'cheese'. See 'cheese'.
Chelal; one of those named in Ezra among the men who had taken strange wives.
Chelluh; a post-exilic name among those who had taken strange wives.
Chelub; a Judahite name in the genealogies.
Chelubai; another genealogical spelling of Caleb in the line of Judah.
Chemarims; idolatrous priests denounced in the Old Testament.
Chemosh; the national god of Moab, a false deity condemned in Scripture.
Chenaanah; the father of Zedekiah the false prophet in the days of Ahab.
Chenani; one of the singers or Levites in the post-exilic community.
Chenaniah; a chief of the Levites who oversaw the bearing of the ark and temple song.
Chephar-Haammonai; a town in the inheritance of Benjamin near the hill-country settlements.
Chephar-haammonai.note: Location: Often identified with Khirbet Kafr Ana.

definitions: Chephirah; a Gibeonite town later counted within Benjamin's inheritance.
Chephirah.note: Location: Commonly identified with Khirbet el Kefireh.

definitions: Cheran; a descendant in the Horite genealogies.
Cherethims; a people linked with the Philistine coast, and in some passages the Cherethites who served as David's guard.
Cherethites; a people associated with the Philistine sphere, probably of Cretan origin, and also the royal guard under David.
Cherethites.note: Location: Usually located in or identified with Crete.

definitions: Cherish; to warm, nourish, or treat with tenderness and care.
Cherished; past tense or past participial form of 'cherish'. See 'cherish'.
Cherisheth; older third-person singular form of 'cherish'. See 'cherish'.
Cherith; the brook where Elijah was hidden and fed by ravens during the drought.
Cherith.note: Location: Often identified with Wadi al Yabis.

definitions: Cherub; one of the exalted heavenly beings associated with the throne and glory of God.
Cherubim; the winged heavenly beings so named in Scripture.
Chesalon; a town on the border of Judah, marking part of the western hill-country line.
Chesed; a son of Nahor named in the genealogies of Genesis.
Chesil; a southern town in the Simeonite and Judean lists, probably related to Bethul in parallel records.
Chesil.note: Location: Commonly identified with Khirbet ar Ras.

definitions: Chesnut; a tree or wood so named in Scripture.
Chest; a box or coffer, and in some uses the breast.
Chests; boxes, coffers, or breasts.
Chesulloth; a border town of Issachar near the Tabor region.
Chewed; past tense or past participial form of 'chew'. See 'chew'.
Cheweth; older third-person singular form of 'chew'. See 'chew'.
Chezib; a Judahite town associated with Shelah in Genesis, probably the same place elsewhere called Achzib in Judah.
Chezib.note: Location: Commonly identified with Tel el Beida.

definitions: Plural form of 'chicken'. See 'chicken'.
Chide; to rebuke, scold, or contend with.
Chiding; present participial form of 'chide', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'chide'.
Chidon; the threshingfloor or place-name connected with the bringing up of the ark.
Chief; foremost, principal, or highest in rank, place, or importance.
As a noun it may mean the leading person or head of a group.
Chiefest; foremost, principal, or greatest in rank among those compared.
In a chief manner or degree.
A child; a young person or offspring.
Childbearing; present participial form of 'childbear', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'childbear'.
the state or period of being a child.
Childish; belonging to a child, immature, or weak like a child.
Childless; having no children.
Sons and daughters; offspring.
Often also descendants, members of a people, or those under instruction or care.
Worthless, lawless, or vile persons.
Chileab; David's son by Abigail, elsewhere called Daniel.
Chilion; the son of Elimelech and Naomi, husband of Orpah.
Chilmad; a district named by Ezekiel among the trading partners of Tyre.
Chilmad.note: Location: Often located in or identified with Tell Hagmatana, though the exact identification is debated.

definitions: Chimham; the son of Barzillai, honoured by David and connected with the lodging-place near Bethlehem.
Chimney; an opening or vent through which smoke passes out.
Chinnereth; an older biblical name for the Sea of Galilee and also for the settlement or district beside it.
Chinnereth.note: Location: Commonly identified with Tel Kinrot.

definitions: Chinneroth; an older name for the Sea of Galilee or for the district around it.
Chinneroth.note: Location: Usually identified with Sea of Galilee.

definitions: an island in the Aegean Sea on Paul's voyage.
Chisleu; the ninth month of the Hebrew year.
Chislon; the father of Elidad, prince of Benjamin.
Chisloth-Tabor; a border town near Mount Tabor in the territory of Zebulun.
Chittim; a name used for Cyprus and sometimes more broadly for western maritime lands or peoples.
Chiun; a name connected with idolatrous star-worship in Amos.
Chloe; a woman whose household brought news to Paul about the Corinthians.
Chode; an old past tense of chide, meaning rebuked or contended.
Choice; selection, or that which is select and excellent.
Choicest; older second-person singular form of 'choice', used with 'thou'. See 'choice'.
Choke; to stifle, crowd out, or stop the breath or growth.
Choked; past tense or past participial form of 'choke'. See 'choke'.
Choler; anger, wrath, or fiery temper.
Choose; to select, prefer, or appoint.
Choosest; older second-person singular form of 'choose', used with 'thou'. See 'choose'.
Chooseth; older third-person singular form of 'choose'. See 'choose'.
Choosing; present participial form of 'choose', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'choose'.
Chop; to cut with repeated strokes.
Chor-Ashan; a southern town, probably another form related to Ashan in the Negev lists.
Chor-ashan.note: Location: Commonly identified with Khirbet Asan.

definitions: Chorazin; a Galilean town reproved by Jesus for unbelief despite mighty works done there.
Chorazin.note: Location: North of the Sea of Galilee near Capernaum, commonly identified with Khirbet Karraza.

definitions: Chose; the past tense of choose.
Selected, picked out, or appointed by choice.
Chozeba; a Judahite town in the genealogies, probably related to Chezib in parallel naming traditions.
Chozeba.note: Location: Commonly identified with Tel el Beida.

definitions: Christ; the Anointed One, the royal and saving title of the promised Messiah, fulfilled supremely in the Lord Jesus.
Christian; one belonging to Christ and known as His follower.
In the New Testament the name marks those publicly identified with Jesus and His disciples.
Christians; those known as followers of Christ.
Plural form of 'christ'. See 'christ'.
Plural form of 'chronicle'. See 'chronicle'.
Chrysolite; a precious stone mentioned among the jewels of Scripture.
Chrysoprasus; , or Chrysoprase (Ezek.
Chub; a people named with Egypt's allies in Ezekiel's prophecy, though not certainly identified.
Chun; a Syrian city from which David took much bronze after defeating Hadadezer.
Chun.note: Location: Often identified with Ras Baalbek.

definitions: The church; the called assembly or congregation of God's people.
Plural form of 'church'. See 'church'.
Churl; a rude, harsh, or miserly man.
Churlish; harsh, rude, surly, or miserly in temper.
Churning; present participial form of 'churn', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'churn'.
Chushan-Rishathaim; the Mesopotamian king who oppressed Israel in the days before Othniel delivered them.
Chuza; the steward of Herod, husband of Joanna.
Cieled; past tense or past participial form of 'ciel'. See 'ciel'.
Cieling; present participial form of 'ciel', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'ciel'.
Cilicia; the southeastern region of Asia Minor, notable as the home district of Tarsus and Saul.
It is used chiefly as a regional designation in apostolic history.
Cilicia.note: Location: In the southeast corner of Asia Minor, around Tarsus and the northeastern Mediterranean coast.

definitions: Cinnamon; a fragrant spice from aromatic bark.
Cinneroth; an older biblical spelling for Chinneroth, the Sea of Galilee or the district adjoining it.
Cinneroth.note: Location: Commonly identified with Tel Kinrot.

definitions: Circle; a round compass, circuit, horizon, or ring.
Circuit; a going around, a round of travel, or a surrounding course.
Plural form of 'circuit'. See 'circuit'.
Circumcise; to cut off the foreskin as the covenant sign appointed to Abraham and his seed.
Circumcised; past tense or past participial form of 'circumcise'. See 'circumcise'.
Circumcising; present participial form of 'circumcise', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'circumcise'.
The cutting away of the foreskin as the covenant sign given to Abraham and his seed.

Coz; a Judahite ancestor named in the genealogies.
Cozbi; the Midianitish woman slain with Zimri in the matter of Baal-peor.
Crackling; present participial form of 'crackle', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'crackle'.
Plural form of 'cracknel'. See 'cracknel'.
Skill in making or doing.
Often also cunning, artifice, or deceitful skill.
The state, quality, or condition of being craftines.
A craftsman; a skilled workman in some art, trade, or manual craft.
Craftsmen; skilled workmen in arts, trades, or manual crafts.
Crafty; skilful in cunning or deception, subtle in a wrongful sense.
Crag; a steep rugged rock or cliff.
Crane; a long-necked bird named in Scripture among the birds.
Crashing; present participial form of 'crash', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'crash'.
Craved; past tense or past participial form of 'crave'. See 'crave'.
Craveth; older third-person singular form of 'crave'. See 'crave'.
Create; to bring into being, appoint, or form by purposeful act, especially of God's creative power.
Created; past tense or past participial form of 'create'. See 'create'.
Createth; older third-person singular form of 'create'. See 'create'.
the act of creating, or what has been created.
Creator; one who creates, and chiefly in Scripture the title of God as maker of all things.
A created thing; that which God has made.
Sometimes used broadly of mankind or the whole creation.
Creditor; one to whom money or debt is owed.
Plural form of 'creditor'. See 'creditor'.
Creek; a small inlet, stream, or narrow arm of water.
Creeps; moves low, slowly, or close to the ground.
Creeping; present participial form of 'creep', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'creep'.
Crept; past tense of creep, meaning moved slowly, crawled, or advanced stealthily.
Crescens; a fellow worker mentioned by Paul as having gone to Galatia.
Crete; the large Mediterranean island visited in the Acts narrative and later associated with Titus.
Crete.note: Location: A large island in the Mediterranean south of the Aegean, commonly identified with modern Crete.

definitions: Plural form of 'crete'. See 'crete'.
Cretians; people of Crete, or those connected with that island.
Crew; past tense of crow, meaning gave the cry of a cock.
In later usage also a band or company, though that sense is not the common KJV one.
Crib; a stall, manger, or place where fodder is laid for cattle.
Cried out; called aloud, proclaimed, or wept with a loud voice.
Plural form of 'cry'. See 'cry'.
Criest; thou criest; you cry out, call aloud, or make earnest appeal.
Cries; calls aloud; makes urgent appeal.
Often used of fervent pleading, proclamation, or complaint.
Crime; serious offence, guilt, or punishable wrongdoing.
Plural form of 'crime'. See 'crime'.
Crimson; a deep rich red colour, or of that colour.
Cripple; one disabled in limb or movement.
As a verb, to lame or disable.
Crisping; present participial form of 'crisp', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'crisp'.
Pins or instruments used in dressing and curling the hair.
Crispus; the chief ruler of the synagogue at Corinth who believed on the Lord.
Crookbackt; old spelling of crookbacked, bent or hunchbacked.
As a noun, one so afflicted.
Crooked; past tense or past participial form of 'crook'. See 'crook'.
Crop; the gathered produce of the field, or the forepart of a bird's throat.
Cropped; cut short, clipped, or gathered as a crop.
The instrument of crucifixion.
Also the shame, suffering, or burden connected with following Christ.
Crossway; a crossing road or place where ways meet.
Crouch; to stoop low, bend down, or lie close to the ground.
Croucheth; older third-person singular form of 'crouch'. See 'crouch'.
Crow; to utter the cry of a cock.
As a noun, the cock's cry or the bird itself in some uses.
A royal headpiece or emblem of honor.
Also a reward, dignity, or mark of triumph.
Crowned; past tense or past participial form of 'crown'. See 'crown'.
Crownedst; means thou didst crown.
Crownest; older second-person singular form of 'crown', used with 'thou'. See 'crown'.
Crowneth; older third-person singular form of 'crown'. See 'crown'.
Crowning; present participial form of 'crown', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'crown'.
To put to death by fastening to a cross.
Figuratively, to put to death or renounce by severe mortification.
Cruel; severe, harsh, pitiless, or causing pain without mercy.
In a cruel manner or degree.
Cruelty; hardhearted severity, harsh treatment, or delight in causing pain.
Plural form of 'crumb'. See 'crumb'.
Cruse; a small vessel or jar, especially for oil or water.
Crush; to break, bruise, press down, or destroy by force.
Crushed; past tense or past participial form of 'crush'. See 'crush'.
Crying; present participial form of 'cry', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'cry'.
a clear, hard mineral or glasslike substance.
Cubit; an ancient measure of length based on the forearm.
Cubits; measures of length based on the forearm.
Cuckow; the bird so named in older spelling, listed among birds in Scripture.
Plural form of 'cucumber'. See 'cucumber'.
Cud; the food brought up again to be chewed by clean beasts that chew the cud.
Cumbered; past tense or past participial form of 'cumber'. See 'cumber'.
Cumbereth; older third-person singular form of 'cumber'. See 'cumber'.
a burden, hindrance, or encumbrance.
Cumi; Aramaic for arise, preserved in the phrase Talitha cumi.
Cummin; an aromatic seed or herb used as a spice.
Cunning; present participial form of 'cunning', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'cunning'.
In a cunning manner or degree.
A cup is a vessel for drinking.
In Scripture it often also signifies one's appointed portion, whether of blessing, wrath, sorrow, or joy.
Cupbearer; one who serves drink to a king or ruler, often a trusted court officer.
Plural form of 'cupbearer'. See 'cupbearer'.
Plural form of 'cup'. See 'cup'.
Curdled; past tense or past participial form of 'curdle'. See 'curdle'.
healing or care; also to heal or restore.
Cured; past tense or past participial form of 'cure'. See 'cure'.
Plural form of 'cure'. See 'cure'.
Skillfully made; artfully wrought; finely worked.
In some contexts, occupied with magical or occult arts.
Magical arts or occult practices.
Current; running, flowing, or in present course.
As a noun, a stream or flow.
A sentence or speaking of evil against one; a state of being under divine or judicial displeasure.
To curse; to speak evil against or invoke judgment upon.
Cursedst; means thou didst curse.
Plural form of 'curse'. See 'curse'.
Cursest; thou cursest; you curse or speak evil against.
Cursing; present participial form of 'curse', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'curse'.
Plural form of 'cursing'. See 'cursing'.
A curtain is a hanging cloth or covering, especially one used in a tent, chamber, or sacred structure.
Plural form of 'curtain'. See 'curtain'.
Cush; the son of Ham and ancestor of the Cushite peoples.
Cushan; a people or region named poetically by Habakkuk, likely connected with Cushite or Midianite territory.
Cushan.note: Location: Often located in or identified with northwestern Arabia, though the exact identification is debated.

definitions: Cushi; the name of men in Scripture, including the father of the prophet Zephaniah and the bearer of tidings to David.
Custody; keeping, guard, or the state of being held under watch.
Custom; habit, usual practice, or tax and toll collected by authority.
Plural form of 'custom'. See 'custom'.
Cuth; the city from which Assyria transplanted colonists into Samaria; related to Cuthah in the same history.
Cuth.note: Location: Commonly identified with Tell Ibrahim.

definitions: Cuthah; the city from which Assyria brought colonists into Samaria after the exile of Israel.
Cuthah.note: Location: Commonly identified with Tell Ibrahim.

definitions: Cuttest; older second-person singular form of 'cut', used with 'thou'. See 'cut'.
Cuts; severs; divides; cuts off.
Cutting; present participial form of 'cut', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'cut'.
Plural form of 'cutting'. See 'cutting'.
a metallic instrument sounded by striking.
Plural form of 'cymbal'. See 'cymbal'.
a cypress tree or its wood.
Cyprus; the large island in the eastern Mediterranean associated with Barnabas and early missionary travel.
The name may denote the island or its inhabitants.
Cyprus.note: Location: A large island in the eastern Mediterranean south of Asia Minor.

definitions: Cyrene; the leading Greek city of North Africa associated with Simon of Cyrene and a Jewish community in the New Testament.
Cyrenian; an inhabitant of Cyrene in North Africa, as in the case of Simon of Cyrene.
Cyrenians; inhabitants of Cyrene in North Africa, including Jews and converts connected with the New Testament story.
the Roman governor Quirinius, associated with the taxing in Luke 2.
Cyrus; a man named in Scripture.
D; the fourth letter of the alphabet.
Dabareh; a Levitical town in Issachar, better known in a related form as Daberath.
Dabareh.note: Location: Often identified with Horvat Devora.

definitions: Dabbasheth; a place on the border of Zebulun marking part of that tribe's inheritance line.
Dabbasheth.note: Location: Often identified with Tel Shem, though the exact identification is debated.

definitions: Daberath; a Levitical town on the border of Issachar near Mount Tabor.
Daberath.note: Location: Often identified with Horvat Devora.

definitions: Dagger; a short sword or stabbing weapon.
Dagon; a Philistine false god whose worship appears in conflict with the worship of the LORD.
Daily; every day, or belonging to each day.
Plural form of 'dainty'. See 'dainty'.
Dainty; delicate, choice, or pleasant to the taste.
Dalaiah; the name of men in post-exilic and prophetic contexts, including one who urged Jeremiah to be hidden.
Dale; a valley or low open place.
Dalmanutha; the lakeside district to which Jesus came after feeding the four thousand.
Dalmanutha.note: Location: Near the Sea of Galilee, though the exact site is uncertain.

definitions: Dalmatia; the Adriatic region to which Titus is said to have gone in Paul's later ministry.
Dalphon; one of the sons of Haman slain in the days of Esther.
Dam; the mother of a young bird or beast.
loss, injury, or harm; also to harm or impair.
Damaris; a woman at Athens who believed after Paul's discourse on Mars' hill.
Plural form of 'damascene'. See 'damascene'.
Damascus; the ancient capital of Syria, prominent in the prophets and in Saul's conversion narrative.
Destructive and worthy of condemnation.
Not a mere exclamation, but carrying the sense of ruinous guilt.
Condemnation under judgment.
The word may denote the judicial sentence itself, not only the final state.
Damned; past tense or past participial form of 'damn'. See 'damn'.
Damsel; a young woman or maiden.
Plural form of 'damsel'. See 'damsel'.
Dan; the son of Jacob, the tribe descended from him, or the city so named.
Dan-Jaan; a northern locality named in David's census route, apparently in the region of Dan toward Sidon.
Dan-jaan.note: Location: Commonly identified with Tel Dan.

definitions: measured movement to music; also to move in that way.
Danced; past tense or past participial form of 'dance'. See 'dance'.
Plural form of 'dance'. See 'dance'.
Dancing; present participial form of 'dance', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'dance'.
Dandled; past tense or past participial form of 'dandle'. See 'dandle'.
Danger; exposure to harm, punishment, or peril.
Dangerous; full of danger, hazardous, or threatening harm.
Daniel; the prophet of that name, and in some genealogies another person so called.
Danites; the members of the tribe descended from Dan.
Dannah; a hill-country town of Judah named among its settlements.
Dannah.note: Location: Sometimes identified with Khirbet Zanuta.

definitions: Dara; one of the wise men whose wisdom was surpassed by Solomon.
Darda; another biblical spelling of Dara, one of the wise men mentioned in comparison with Solomon.
Dare; to venture boldly, or to have courage to do a thing.
Darius; the royal name of rulers mentioned in Scripture, especially kings of the Medes and Persians.
Dark; without light, obscure, or spiritually gloomy.
Darken; to make dark, obscure, or dim.
Darkened; past tense or past participial form of 'darken'. See 'darken'.
Darkeneth; older third-person singular form of 'darken'. See 'darken'.
Darkish; somewhat dark.
In a dark manner or degree.
Lack of light.
Often spiritual blindness, ignorance, evil, or judgment.
Darkon; the head of a family of Nethinims or temple servants.
Darling; present participial form of 'darling', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'darling'.
Dart; a light spear or missile, often used figuratively of sharp or fiery assaults.
Plural form of 'dart'. See 'dart'.
Dash; to strike violently, break by sudden force, or throw down.
Dashed; past tense or past participial form of 'dash'. See 'dash'.
Dasheth; older third-person singular form of 'dash'. See 'dash'.
Dathan; the Reubenite who joined Korah in rebellion against Moses and Aaron.
Daub; to smear over, plaster, or cover superficially.
Daubed; past tense or past participial form of 'daub'. See 'daub'.
Daubing; present participial form of 'daub', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'daub'.
A daughter; a female child, descendant, or one spoken of under that relation.
Daughters; female children, descendants, or women spoken of under that relation.
David; the son of Jesse and king of Israel, and in some passages the royal line or kingdom connected with him.
Dawn; the first breaking of day.
Dawning; present participial form of 'dawn', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'dawn'.
A day; the time of light, or a measured period or occasion.
Days; periods of time or occasions.
Daysman; an umpire, mediator, or one who can lay a hand upon both parties.
Dayspring; present participial form of 'dayspring', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'dayspring'.
Daytime; the lighted part of the day.
A servant or minister in the church, especially one entrusted with practical service.
Dead; without life, lifeless, or spiritually inactive.
In a dead manner or degree.
The state, quality, or condition of being deadnes.
Deaf; unable to hear, or unwilling to hear.
Dealer; one who acts, trades, or deals with others.
Plural form of 'dealer'. See 'dealer'.
Dealest; older second-person singular form of 'deal', used with 'thou'. See 'deal'.
Dealeth; older third-person singular form of 'deal'. See 'deal'.
Dealing; present participial form of 'deal', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'deal'.
Plural form of 'dealing'. See 'dealing'.
Plural form of 'deal'. See 'deal'.
Dealt; the past tense and past participle of deal.
Dear; beloved, precious, costly, or grievous.
In a dear manner or degree.
Dearth; scarcity, especially of food, or famine.
The end of bodily life; departure of life from the body.
The word may also describe condemnation, separation, or deadly ruin.
Debase; to bring low, degrade, or make mean.
Debate; strife, contention, or dispute.
Plural form of 'debate'. See 'debate'.
Debir; a person-name in Judahite genealogy, distinct from the better-known place-name.
Deborah; the prophetess and judge who led Israel in the days of Barak, and also the nurse of Rebekah.
Debt; that which is owed, whether money, duty, or obligation.
Debtor; one who owes another.
Plural form of 'debtor'. See 'debtor'.
Plural form of 'debt'. See 'debt'.
the confederation of ten Hellenistic cities east and southeast of Galilee.
Decay; decline, wasting away, or corruption.
Decayed; past tense or past participial form of 'decay'. See 'decay'.
Decayeth; older third-person singular form of 'decay'. See 'decay'.
Decease; death, departure, or the act of dying.
Deceased; past tense or past participial form of 'decease'. See 'decease'.
Fraud, guile, or hidden false dealing.
Deceitful; false, treacherous, or likely to mislead.
In a deceitful manner or degree.
The state, quality, or condition of being deceitfulnes.
Plural form of 'deceit'. See 'deceit'.
The state, quality, or condition of being deceivablenes.
To mislead, delude, or cause one to believe what is false.
Deceiver; one who misleads or beguiles.
Plural form of 'deceiver'. See 'deceiver'.
Deceiving; present participial form of 'deceive', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'deceive'.
Plural form of 'deceiving'. See 'deceiving'.
In a decent manner or degree.
Decided; past tense or past participial form of 'decide'. See 'decide'.
a settled determination, judgment, or conclusion.
Deck; to adorn, clothe, or array with ornament.
Decked; past tense or past participial form of 'deck'. See 'deck'.
Deckedst; means thou didst adorn or array.
Deckest; older second-person singular form of 'deck', used with 'thou'. See 'deck'.
Decketh; older third-person singular form of 'deck'. See 'deck'.
a formal statement or proclamation.
To make known plainly, proclaim, or show forth.
An older third-person form meaning 'declares' or 'makes known.'
Declaring; present participial form of 'declare', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'declare'.
Decline; to turn aside, sink downward, or refuse.
Declined; turned aside, sank downward, or refused.
Declineth; means turns aside, sinks, or refuses.
Decrease; lessening, diminution, or abatement.
Decreased; past tense or past participial form of 'decrease'. See 'decrease'.
A decree is an authoritative order, ordinance, or formal determination issued by one in power.
Decreed; past tense or past participial form of 'decree'. See 'decree'.
Plural form of 'decree'. See 'decree'.
Dedan; the name of men in early genealogies, and ancestor of peoples or trading groups bearing the name.
Dedanim; traders or descendants associated with Dedan and Arabian commerce in prophetic texts.
Dedanim.note: Location: Commonly identified with Al Khuraybah.

definitions: Dedicate; to set apart, devote, or consecrate for a purpose.
Dedicated; past tense or past participial form of 'dedicate'. See 'dedicate'.
Dedicating; present participial form of 'dedicate', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'dedicate'.
the act of setting apart, devoting, or formally opening something.
Deed; an act, work, exploit, or thing done.
Acts, works, or actions done.
Deemed; past tense or past participial form of 'deem'. See 'deem'.
The deep; great depth, especially the sea or abyss, and also deep in measure or thought.
Deeper; more deep, more hidden, or more profound.
In a deep manner or degree.
The state, quality, or condition of being deepnes.
Plural form of 'deep'. See 'deep'.
Deer; a hart-like or gazelle-like animal.
Defamed; past tense or past participial form of 'defame'. See 'defame'.
Defaming; present participial form of 'defame', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'defame'.
Defeat; overthrow, frustration, or being smitten down.
protection, resistance, or an answer made in support of oneself.
Defenced; past tense or past participial form of 'defence'. See 'defence'.
Defend; to protect, maintain, or guard against attack.
Defended; past tense or past participial form of 'defend'. See 'defend'.
Defendest; older second-person singular form of 'defend', used with 'thou'. See 'defend'.
Defending; present participial form of 'defend', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'defend'.
Defer; to put off, delay, or yield to another.
Deferred; put off, delayed, or yielded.
Deferreth; means puts off, delays, or yields.
Defied; past tense or past participial form of 'defy'. See 'defy'.
Made unclean, polluted, or stained.
Defiledst; means thou didst defile.
Defileth; older third-person singular form of 'defile'. See 'defile'.
To deprive by deceit, withhold what is due, or wrongfully overreach.
Defrauded; past tense or past participial form of 'defraud'. See 'defraud'.
Defy; to challenge boldly, or treat with open contempt.
Degenerate; fallen from a better state, corrupt, or turned aside from right kind.
Degree; rank, measure, stage, or step.
Degrees; ranks, measures, stages, or steps.
Dehavites; one of the eastern peoples settled in Samaria under imperial resettlement.
Dekar; one of Solomon's officers who supplied the king's household.
Delaiah; the official who urged Jehoiakim not to burn Jeremiah's scroll, and also the name of priestly or post-exilic figures.
Delay; postponement, lingering, or slowness.
Delayed; past tense or past participial form of 'delay'. See 'delay'.
Delayeth; older third-person singular form of 'delay'. See 'delay'.
Delectable; delightful, pleasant, or highly pleasing.
Plural form of 'delicacy'. See 'delicacy'.
Delicate; tender, luxurious, dainty, or fine.
In a delicate manner or degree.
The state, quality, or condition of being delicatenes.
Delicates; delicacies, dainties.
In a delicious manner or degree.
Pleasure, joy, or a thing greatly pleasing.
To delight; to take pleasure in or greatly please.
Delightest; older second-person singular form of 'delight', used with 'thou'. See 'delight'.
Delights; takes pleasure in; finds joy in.
Plural form of 'delight'. See 'delight'.
Delightsome; pleasant, lovely, or giving delight.
Delilah; the woman of the valley of Sorek who betrayed Samson to the Philistines.
To rescue, release, or bring safely out.
Also to hand over or commit.
Rescue, release, or setting free from danger or bondage.
Plural form of 'deliverance'. See 'deliverance'.
Deliveredst; means thou didst deliver.
Deliverer; one who rescues, saves, or sets free.
Deliverest; older second-person singular form of 'deliver', used with 'thou'. See 'deliver'.
Delivering; present participial form of 'deliver', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'deliver'.
Delivery; rescue, release, or the act of giving over.
deception or a strong false belief.
Plural form of 'delusion'. See 'delusion'.
Demand; to ask for with authority, require, or claim.
Demanded; past tense or past participial form of 'demand'. See 'demand'.
Demas; a companion of Paul who later forsook him, loving this present world.
Demetrius; the name of men in the New Testament, especially the Ephesian silversmith and the brother commended by John.
proof, clear display, or manifest showing.
Den; a lair, cave, or hidden place, especially of wild beasts.
Denounce; to proclaim against, threaten, or declare judgment.
Plural form of 'den'. See 'den'.
To refuse, reject, disclaim, or declare not to be so.
Denying; present participial form of 'deny', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'deny'.
To go away, withdraw, or leave.
At times to turn aside from a course.
Departing; present participial form of 'depart', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'depart'.
going away, removal, or death.
Deposed; past tense or past participial form of 'depose'. See 'depose'.
Deprived; past tense or past participial form of 'deprive'. See 'deprive'.
Depth; deepness, the abyss, or profoundness.
Depths; deep places, abysses, or profound things.
Deputed; past tense or past participial form of 'depute'. See 'depute'.
Plural form of 'deputy'. See 'deputy'.
Deputy; one acting in another’s place, especially a governor or subordinate ruler.
Derbe; a city of Lycaonia visited by Paul in his missionary journeys.
The name chiefly appears as a stage in gospel travel and church planting.
Derbe.note: Location: In Lycaonia in the interior of Asia Minor; the exact site is debated, often linked with Kerti Hüyük.

definitions: Deride; to mock, laugh to scorn, or treat with contempt.
Derided; past tense or past participial form of 'deride'. See 'deride'.
mocking contempt or scorn.
Descend; to go down, come down, or pass by inheritance.
Descended; past tense or past participial form of 'descend'. See 'descend'.
Descendeth; older third-person singular form of 'descend'. See 'descend'.
Descending; present participial form of 'descend', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'descend'.
Descent; a going down, a downward slope, or lineage by family line.
Describe; to trace out, mark out, or declare by words.
Described; past tense or past participial form of 'describe'. See 'describe'.
Describeth; older third-person singular form of 'describe'. See 'describe'.
an account given in words, or the act of describing.
Descry; to catch sight of from afar or discern.
A desert; a barren, dry, or thinly inhabited tract of land.
It may also mean a lonely or waste place apart from settled cultivation.
Plural form of 'desert'. See 'desert'.
Deserve; to be worthy of, whether good or ill.
Deserveth; older third-person singular form of 'deserve'. See 'deserve'.
Deserving; present participial form of 'deserve', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'deserve'.
Desirable; worthy to be desired, pleasing, or attractive.
Wish, longing, or earnest inclination toward a thing.
To desire; to long for, seek, or wish for.
Desiredst; means thou didst desire.
Desirest; older second-person singular form of 'desire', used with 'thou'. See 'desire'.
Desires; longs for; seeks after with inward inclination.
Desiring; present participial form of 'desire', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'desire'.
Desirous; eager for, wishing for, or inclined toward something.
Laid waste, lonely, bereft, or emptied.
Plural form of 'desolation'. See 'desolation'.
Despair; loss of hope, hopelessness, or utter despondency.
Despaired; past tense or past participial form of 'despair'. See 'despair'.
Desperate; exceedingly dangerous, reckless, or beyond ordinary hope.
In a desperate manner or degree.
Regarded as of little worth; scorned or treated contemptuously.
Plural form of 'despiser'. See 'despiser'.
Despisest; older second-person singular form of 'despise', used with 'thou'. See 'despise'.
Despises; regards as little, vile, or unworthy.
Despising; present participial form of 'despise', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'despise'.
Despite; contempt, scorn, malicious injury, or in spite of.
Despiteful; malicious, spiteful, or acting with scornful ill will.
In a despiteful manner or degree.
Destitute; lacking, needy, or deprived of what is necessary.
Overthrown, ruined, slain, or brought to nothing.
Destroyer; one who destroys, or an agent of destruction and judgment.
Plural form of 'destroyer'. See 'destroyer'.
Destroyest; thou destroyest; you ruin, overthrow, or bring to nothing.
Destroyeth; older third-person singular form of 'destroy'. See 'destroy'.
Destroying; present participial form of 'destroy', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'destroy'.
Ruin, overthrow, or bringing to nothing.
It may describe wasting loss and perdition, not always immediate annihilation.
Plural form of 'destruction'. See 'destruction'.
Detain; to hold back, keep, or delay.
Detained; past tense or past participial form of 'detain'. See 'detain'.
Determinate; fixed, appointed, or definitely settled.
firmness of purpose or a settled decision.
Determine; to decide, appoint, or settle.
Determined; past tense or past participial form of 'determine'. See 'determine'.
Detest; older second-person singular form of 'detest', used with 'thou'. See 'detest'.
Detestable; hateful, abominable, or worthy of detestation.
Deuel; a man named in Scripture, father of Zipporah.
A plan, contrivance, or purpose formed in the mind.
Devil; the great adversary, Satan; in some passages also a false accuser or slanderer.
Devilish; belonging to demons or marked by their spirit.
Devils; evil spirits, false accusers, or slanderers as context requires.
To plan, imagine, or frame in the mind.
Devises; plans, contrives, or forms in the mind.
Devote; to give over, set apart, or dedicate wholly.
Devoted; past tense or past participial form of 'devote'. See 'devote'.
Plural form of 'devotion'. See 'devotion'.
Devoured; past tense or past participial form of 'devour'. See 'devour'.
Devourer; one who consumes, spoils, or destroys.
Devourest; older second-person singular form of 'devour', used with 'thou'. See 'devour'.
Devoureth; older third-person singular form of 'devour'. See 'devour'.
Devouring; present participial form of 'devour', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'devour'.
Devout; pious, reverent, and earnest in religion.
Dew; the moisture that falls gently in the night or early morning, often an emblem of blessing and freshness.
Diadem; a royal crown or headband of rule.
a marked face or plate for measuring, especially on a sundial.
Diamond; a very hard precious stone, or a stone of similar cutting use.
Diana; the Roman name for the goddess worshipped at Ephesus, identified with Artemis.
Diblaim; the father of Gomer in the opening of Hosea.
Diblath; a place connected with the Moabite region in Ezekiel's prophecy, likely related to Diblathaim.
Dibon; a chief Moabite city, important in the history and prophecies concerning Moab.
Dibon.note: Location: Commonly identified with Tall Dhiban.

definitions: Dibon-Gad; the wilderness-station or Transjordan designation of Dibon in the encampment lists east of the Jordan.
Dibon-gad.note: Location: Commonly identified with Tall Dhiban.

definitions: Dibri; the father of Shelomith in the law case of Leviticus.
The past tense of do.
Diddest; older second-person singular form of 'did', used with 'thou'. See 'did'.
Didst; the older second-person singular past of do, meaning thou didst.
Didymus; a surname meaning twin, applied especially to Thomas.
The past tense of die.
Diest; older second-person singular form of 'die', used with 'thou'. See 'die'.
Diet; appointed food or daily provision.
Dieth; older third-person singular form of 'die'. See 'die'.
Differ; to be unlike, distinct, or at variance.
distinction, disagreement, or unlikeness.
Plural form of 'difference'. See 'difference'.
Differeth; older third-person singular form of 'differ'. See 'differ'.
Differing; present participial form of 'differ', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'differ'.
Dig; to break up the ground or make a pit, trench, or well.
Digged; past tense or past participial form of 'dig'. See 'dig'.
Diggedst; means thou didst dig.
Diggeth; older third-person singular form of 'dig'. See 'dig'.
Plural form of 'dignity'. See 'dignity'.
honor, worth, or elevated rank.
Diklah; one of the sons of Joktan in the early genealogies.
Dilean; a town in the lowland of Judah named among the Shephelah settlements.
Dilean.note: Location: Often identified with Tel Nagila.

definitions: Steady care, earnest effort, and careful attention.
Diligent; careful, steady, and earnest in effort.
In a diligent manner or degree.
Dim; not bright, faint, or obscure to sight.
Diminish; to make less, reduce, or take away from.
Diminished; past tense or past participial form of 'diminish'. See 'diminish'.
Diminishing; present participial form of 'diminish', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'diminish'.
Dimnah; a town assigned to the Levites, likely in Zebulun's region.
Dimnah.note: Location: Often identified with Horbat Rumah.

definitions: The state, quality, or condition of being dimnes.
Dimon; the prophetic name used for Dibon in Isaiah's oracle against Moab.
Dimon.note: Location: Often identified with Tall Dhiban.

definitions: Dimonah; a town in the south of Judah named among the Negev settlements.
Dimonah.note: Location: In the southern territory of Judah, though the exact site is uncertain.

definitions: Dinah; the daughter of Jacob and Leah.
Dinaites; one of the foreign peoples named among those settled in Samaria.
Dine; to take the principal meal.
Dined; past tense or past participial form of 'dine'. See 'dine'.
Dinhabah; the city of Bela, one of the early kings of Edom.
Dinhabah.note: Location: Sometimes identified with Khirbet el Muhatta.

definitions: Dinner; a meal, often the chief meal of the day.
Dionysius; the Areopagite at Athens who believed after hearing Paul.
Diotrephes; the self-willed church leader censured in 3 John.
Dip; to plunge briefly into liquid, or immerse in part.
Dipped; past tense or past participial form of 'dip'. See 'dip'.
Dippeth; older third-person singular form of 'dip'. See 'dip'.
Direct; to guide, order, or point straight toward an end.
Directed; past tense or past participial form of 'direct'. See 'direct'.
Directeth; older third-person singular form of 'direct'. See 'direct'.
guidance, command, or a line in which something moves.
In a direct manner or degree.
Dirt; foul matter, mud, or uncleanness.
Disallow; to reject, refuse, or treat as unworthy.
Disallowed; past tense or past participial form of 'disallow'. See 'disallow'.
To make void; to set aside; to cancel or invalidate.
Disannulleth; older third-person singular form of 'disannul'. See 'disannul'.
Disannulling; present participial form of 'disannul', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'disannul'.
Disappoint; to frustrate, defeat expectation, or hinder from intended success.
Disappointed; past tense or past participial form of 'disappoint'. See 'disappoint'.
Disappointeth; older third-person singular form of 'disappoint'. See 'disappoint'.
To distinguish, perceive, or judge accurately between things.
Discerner; one who distinguishes or judges rightly.
Discerning; present participial form of 'discern', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'discern'.
Discharge; release, performance of duty, or the act of sending forth.
Discharged; released, performed, or sent forth.
A disciple; a learner, follower, or one instructed under a teacher.
In Gospel usage it commonly means a follower of Jesus.
Disciples; learners and followers under a teacher, especially those attached to Christ.
Discipline; training, correction, or ordered instruction.
Disclose; to uncover, reveal, or make known.
Discomfited; past tense or past participial form of 'discomfit'. See 'discomfit'.
rout, defeat, or confusion.
Discontented; past tense or past participial form of 'discontent'. See 'discontent'.
Discontinue; to cease from, break off, or interrupt.
Discord; strife, disagreement, or want of harmony.
to dishearten or deprive of courage.
Discouraged; past tense or past participial form of 'discourage'. See 'discourage'.
Discover; to uncover, reveal, or make bare.
Discovered; past tense or past participial form of 'discover'. See 'discover'.
Discovereth; older third-person singular form of 'discover'. See 'discover'.
Discovering; present participial form of 'discover', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'discover'.
Discreet; prudent, modest, and governed by good judgment.
In a discreet manner or degree.
Sound judgment, wise restraint, and prudent decision.
Disdained; past tense or past participial form of 'disdain'. See 'disdain'.
Disease; sickness, disorder, or bodily infirmity.
Diseased; past tense or past participial form of 'disease'. See 'disease'.
Plural form of 'disease'. See 'disease'.
to mar the form or appearance of.
Disgrace; shame, dishonor, or reproach.
Disguise; changed outward appearance meant to conceal identity.
Disguised; past tense or past participial form of 'disguise'. See 'disguise'.
Disguiseth; older third-person singular form of 'disguise'. See 'disguise'.
Dish; a vessel for food, or the food in it.
Dishan; a Horite name in the genealogies of Seir.
Dishes; vessels for food, or the food they hold.
Dishon; a Horite chief in the genealogies of Seir.
Dishonest; older second-person singular form of 'dishon', used with 'thou'. See 'dishon'.
Dishonesty; want of honesty, false dealing, or shameful conduct.
Dishonour; shame, contempt, or loss of honor.
Dishonourest; older second-person singular form of 'dishonour', used with 'thou'. See 'dishonour'.
Dishonoureth; older third-person singular form of 'dishonour'. See 'dishonour'.
Disinherit; to deprive of inheritance or drive out from possession.
Dismayed; past tense or past participial form of 'dismay'. See 'dismay'.
Dismaying; present participial form of 'dismay', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'dismay'.
Dismissed; past tense or past participial form of 'dismiss'. See 'dismiss'.
Refusal or failure to obey rightful authority.
Disobedient; refusing to obey, rebellious, or unsubmissive.
Disobeyed; past tense or past participial form of 'disobey'. See 'disobey'.
In a disorder manner or degree.
Dispatch; to send away quickly, finish speedily, or put to death.
A stewardship, ordering, or administration committed to one.
Sometimes the ordered arrangement of divine dealings in a given matter.
Disperse; to scatter abroad.
Dispersed; past tense or past participial form of 'disperse'. See 'disperse'.
Plural form of 'dispersion'. See 'dispersion'.
Displayed; past tense or past participial form of 'display'. See 'display'.
Displease; to offend, vex, or fail to satisfy.
Displeased; past tense or past participial form of 'displease'. See 'displease'.
dissatisfaction, offense, or anger.
Disposed; past tense or past participial form of 'dispose'. See 'dispose'.
Disposing; present participial form of 'dispose', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'dispose'.
arrangement, tendency of mind, or ordered appointment.
to drive out and take possession from another.
Dispossessed; past tense or past participial form of 'dispossess'. See 'dispossess'.
Debate, doubtful argument, or contentious reasoning.
Dispute; to reason against, contend, or argue a matter.
Disputed; past tense or past participial form of 'dispute'. See 'dispute'.
Disputer; one given to argument or debate.
Disputing; present participial form of 'dispute', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'dispute'.
Plural form of 'disputing'. See 'disputing'.
Disquiet; disturbance, restlessness, or inward trouble.
Disquieted; past tense or past participial form of 'disquiet'. See 'disquiet'.
The state, quality, or condition of being disquietnes.
Dissembled; past tense or past participial form of 'dissemble'. See 'dissemble'.
Plural form of 'dissembler'. See 'dissembler'.
Dissembleth; older third-person singular form of 'dissemble'. See 'dissemble'.
disagreement producing strife.
concealment of one’s real thoughts; hypocrisy or feigning.
Dissolve; to loose, melt away, or break up.
Dissolved; past tense or past participial form of 'dissolve'. See 'dissolve'.
Dissolvest; older second-person singular form of 'dissolve', used with 'thou'. See 'dissolve'.
Dissolving; present participial form of 'dissolve', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'dissolve'.
Distaff; the staff on which flax or wool is held for spinning.
Distant; far off in place, time, or relation.
Distil; to drop gently, as dew or speech.
difference, separation, or special honor.
In a distinct manner or degree.
Distracted; past tense or past participial form of 'distract'. See 'distract'.
mental disturbance, confusion, or being drawn away.
trouble, anguish, or pressure; also to afflict severely.
Distressed; past tense or past participial form of 'distress'. See 'distress'.
Plural form of 'distress'. See 'distress'.
Distribute; to divide out or give to several persons.
Distributed; past tense or past participial form of 'distribute'. See 'distribute'.
Distributeth; older third-person singular form of 'distribute'. See 'distribute'.
Distributing; present participial form of 'distribute', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'distribute'.
the act of dividing out, giving, or apportioning.
A ditch is a trench or hollow cut in the earth, whether for drainage, boundary, or danger.
Plural form of 'ditch'. See 'ditch'.
Various; several; of different kinds.
Not the same as the modern noun for one who dives.
Diverse; different, various, or several.
Plural form of 'diversity'. See 'diversity'.
Separated, parted, or distributed into portions.
Divider; one who separates, apportions, or judges a division.
Divideth; older third-person singular form of 'divide'. See 'divide'.
Dividing; present participial form of 'divide', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'divide'.
the attempt to discover secret things by omens or occult means.
Plural form of 'divination'. See 'divination'.
Divine; to practice divination or seek hidden knowledge unlawfully; as an adjective, belonging to God.
Plural form of 'diviner'. See 'diviner'.
Divineth; older third-person singular form of 'divine'. See 'divine'.
Divining; present participial form of 'divine', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'divine'.
separation into parts; also discord or a distinct group.
Plural form of 'division'. See 'division'.
Divorce; the putting away of a wife by legal separation.
Divorced; past tense or past participial form of 'divorce'. See 'divorce'.
divorce or formal dismissal from marriage.
Dizahab; a place named in Moses' wilderness setting, probably toward the Red Sea and the Arabah route.
Dizahab.note: Location: Often identified with Ad Duhaybah.

definitions: A doctor; a learned teacher, especially of the law.
In Scripture the term points to one skilled in teaching rather than to a physician.
Doctors; learned teachers, especially of the law.
Teaching, instruction, or that which is taught as truth.
Dodai; one of David's mighty men and captain of the monthly course for the second month, elsewhere linked with Dodo.
Dodanim; a people or branch named among the descendants of Javan.
Dodavah; the father of Eliezer the prophet who spoke against Jehoshaphat.
Dodo; the father of notable warriors in David's lists.
Doeg; the Edomite servant of Saul who slew the priests of Nob.
Doer; one who acts or performs, especially one who practices what is commanded.
Plural form of 'doer'. See 'doer'.
Doest; thou doest; you do.
Doeth; older third-person singular form of 'do'. See 'do'.
Dog; the animal so called, and in figurative use a term of contempt or uncleanness.
Plural form of 'dog'. See 'dog'.
Doing; action, deed, conduct, or what is done.
Plural form of 'doing'. See 'doing'.
Doleful; sorrowful, mournful, or full of grief.
Dominion; rule, authority, or governing power.
Plural form of 'dominion'. See 'dominion'.
Completed, performed, or finished.
A door; the entrance or opening by which one passes in or out.
A doorkeeper; one set to keep the door or gate and control admission.
The word is especially meaningful in temple and house service.
Doorkeepers; those set to keep the door or gate and control admission.
Doors; entrances, leaves of a doorway, or openings by which one enters.
Dophkah; a wilderness-stage station in Israel's journey after leaving the Red Sea region.
Dophkah.note: Location: Often identified with Serabit el Khadim.

definitions: Dor; a coastal Canaanite city later included in Israel's tribal allotments and royal administration.
Dor.note: Location: Commonly identified with Tel Dor.

definitions: Dorcas; the disciple at Joppa known for good works and raised by Peter; her Aramaic name is Tabitha.
Dost; the older second-person singular present of do.
Dote; to be foolishly fond, or to rave foolishly upon a matter.
Doted; past tense or past participial form of 'dote'. See 'dote'.
Doth; the older third-person singular present of do.
Dothan; the place where Joseph was found by his brothers before being sold, and later the scene of Elisha's deliverance.
Dothan.note: Location: Commonly identified with Tel Dotan.

definitions: Doting; present participial form of 'dote', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'dote'.
Double; twofold, paired, or deceitfully divided.
Doubled; made twofold, repeated, or divided in double.
Doubletongued; past tense or past participial form of 'doubletongued'. See 'doubletongued'.
Doubt; uncertainty, hesitation, or wavering.
Doubted; past tense or past participial form of 'doubt'. See 'doubt'.
Doubteth; older third-person singular form of 'doubt'. See 'doubt'.
Doubtful; uncertain, questionable, or full of doubt.
Doubting; present participial form of 'doubt', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'doubt'.
Doubtless; without doubt, surely, or probably.
Plural form of 'doubt'. See 'doubt'.
Dough; kneaded paste before it is baked.
Dove; the gentle bird so called, often used as a figure of innocence, beauty, or the Spirit’s sign.
Plural form of 'dove'. See 'dove'.
Downward; below; to the ground or a lower place.
Downsitting; the act or state of sitting down, as in Psalm 139:2.
Downward; toward a lower place or state.
Dowry; marriage-gift, portion, or price connected with a bride.
Drag; a net drawn through water, or the act of dragging.
Dragging; drawing along, hauling, or using a drag-net.
Dragon; a great serpent-like or monstrous creature, and in Revelation a title of Satan.
Plural form of 'dragon'. See 'dragon'.
Plural form of 'dram'. See 'dram'.
Drank; the past tense of drink.
Draught; a drawing of fish, a pull, or a quantity drunk.
Drave; an older past tense of drive.
Drawer; one who draws, especially water.
Plural form of 'drawer'. See 'drawer'.
Draweth; older third-person singular form of 'draw'. See 'draw'.
Drawing; present participial form of 'draw', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'draw'.
Drawn; the past participle of draw.
Dread; fearful awe, terror, or that which inspires it.
Dreadful; terrible, fearsome, or causing great dread.
A dream; a vision or impression received in sleep.
Dreamed; past tense or past participial form of 'dream'. See 'dream'.
Dreamer; one who dreams, especially one claiming significance from dreams.
Plural form of 'dreamer'. See 'dreamer'.
Dreameth; older third-person singular form of 'dream'. See 'dream'.
Plural form of 'dream'. See 'dream'.
Plural form of 'dreg'. See 'dreg'.
arrangement or attire; also to prepare, trim, or set in order.
Dressed; past tense or past participial form of 'dress'. See 'dress'.
Dresser; one who tends, prunes, or prepares, especially a dresser of vines.
Plural form of 'dresser'. See 'dresser'.
Dresseth; older third-person singular form of 'dress'. See 'dress'.
The past tense of draw; pulled, brought near, or advanced.
Drewest; older second-person singular form of 'drew', used with 'thou'. See 'drew'.
Dried; past tense or past participial form of 'dry'. See 'dry'.
Driedst; means thou didst dry.
Drieth; older third-person singular form of 'dry'. See 'dry'.
Plural form of 'drinker'. See 'drinker'.
Drinks; takes in drink; partakes by drinking.
Drinking; present participial form of 'drink', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'drink'.
Plural form of 'drink'. See 'drink'.
To force onward, thrust out, or cause to move.
As a noun, a driving movement or course.
Driven; the past participle of drive.
Driver; one who drives men, beasts, or a vehicle.
Driveth; older third-person singular form of 'drive'. See 'drive'.
Driving; present participial form of 'drive', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'drive'.
Plural form of 'dromedary'. See 'dromedary'.
Dromedary; a swift riding camel or camel-like beast.
Drop; a small portion of liquid falling or hanging.
Dropped; past tense or past participial form of 'drop'. See 'drop'.
Droppeth; older third-person singular form of 'drop'. See 'drop'.
Dropping; present participial form of 'drop', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'drop'.
Plural form of 'drop'. See 'drop'.
Dropsy; a swelling disease caused by accumulated fluid in the body.
The refuse or base matter separated from metal; worthless impurity.
Drought; dryness from want of rain or moisture.
Drove; a herd or company driven together, or the past tense of drive.
Plural form of 'drove'. See 'drove'.
Drown; to be overwhelmed in water or cause to perish in water.
Drowned; past tense or past participial form of 'drown'. See 'drown'.
The state, quality, or condition of being drowsines.
Drunk; intoxicated with drink, or figuratively overwhelmed.
Drunkard; one given to drunkenness.
Plural form of 'drunkard'. See 'drunkard'.
Drunken; intoxicated, or figuratively reeling and disordered.
The state, quality, or condition of being drunkennes.
Drusilla; the wife of Felix before whom Paul reasoned of righteousness and judgment.
Dry; without moisture, or dried up.
Dryshod; with the feet dry, not passing through water or mire.
Owed, proper, fitting, or appointed to be rendered.
Plural form of 'due'. See 'due'.
A duke; a chief, prince, or head of a clan or tribe.
In the Old Testament the word often answers to an elder tribal ruler rather than to the later European rank.
Dukes; chiefs, princes, or heads of clans or tribes.
Dulcimer; a musical instrument named in Daniel, though its exact form is uncertain.
Dull; slow, blunt, heavy, or lacking quickness and perception.
Dumah; the name of a son of Ishmael, and also of an Edomite line or district associated with his name.
Dumb; unable to speak, or silent.
Dung; excrement, refuse, or filth.
Dungeon; a pit, prison, or deep place of confinement.
Dunghill; a heap of dung or refuse.
Plural form of 'dunghill'. See 'dunghill'.
Dura; a district mentioned in Scripture.
Dura.note: Location: Often identified with Tulul Dura, though the exact identification is debated.

definitions: Durable; lasting, enduring, or able to continue.
Dureth; older third-person singular form of 'dure'. See 'dure'.
An older past form meaning 'dared.'
Dust; fine earth, powder, or the substance to which man returns in death.
Plural form of 'duty'. See 'duty'.
Duty; that which is owed, required, or fitting to be done.
Dwarf; a person of unusually small stature, or a stunted creature.
To abide, live, or remain in a region, house, or condition.
Dwelled; past tense or past participial form of 'dwell'. See 'dwell'.
Plural form of 'dweller'. See 'dweller'.
Dwellest; thou dwellest; you abide, live, or remain in a place.
Abiding place, habitation, or remaining in a place.
Dwellingplace; a place of habitation or abode.
Plural form of 'dwellingplace'. See 'dwellingplace'.
Plural form of 'dwelling'. See 'dwelling'.
Dyed; past tense or past participial form of 'dye'. See 'dye'.
Dying; present participial form of 'die', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'die'.
Every one separately taken.
Eagle; the great bird of prey so called, often an emblem of swiftness, strength, or desolation.
Plural form of 'eagle'. See 'eagle'.
The ear; the organ of hearing, and by extension attention or obedience.
Eared; past tense or past participial form of 'ear'. See 'ear'.
Earing; present participial form of 'ear', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'ear'.
Early; at an early time, soon in the day, beforehand, or belonging to an early time.
A pledge or first part given in assurance that the rest will follow.
A token securing a future full possession.
With sincere force, zeal, and serious intent.
Earneth; older third-person singular form of 'earn'. See 'earn'.
Earring; present participial form of 'ear', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'ear'.
Plural form of 'earring'. See 'earring'.
Ears; the organs of hearing, and by extension attention or understanding.
The earth, land, ground, world, country, or soil underfoot.
Earthen; made of earth or clay, and therefore frail or lowly in nature.
Earthly; belonging to the earth, worldly, or of this present life.
Earthquake; a shaking of the earth, often associated in Scripture with divine visitation or judgment.
Plural form of 'earthquake'. See 'earthquake'.
Earthy; of the earth, earthly in nature, or crude and material.
Ease; rest, freedom from trouble, or comfortable security.
Eased; past tense or past participial form of 'ease'. See 'ease'.
Easier; more easy, less difficult.
In a easi manner or degree.
The east; the quarter where the sun rises.
Easter; in Acts 12:4 a rendering referring to the Passover season.
Toward the east.
Easy; not hard, gentle, or light to bear.
Consumed as food; devoured.
Eater; one that eats, or that which devours.
Plural form of 'eater'. See 'eater'.
Eatest; thou eatest; you eat or partake of food.
Eateth; older third-person singular form of 'eat'. See 'eat'.
Eating; present participial form of 'eat', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'eat'.
Ebal; Mount Ebal near Shechem, and also a personal name in genealogical lists.
Ebed; chiefly remembered in the compound name Ebed-melech, and also used as a personal name in genealogical records.
Ebed-Melech; the Ethiopian servant of the king who rescued Jeremiah from the dungeon.
Eben-Ezer; the place of conflict with the Philistines, later memorialized by Samuel with the stone of help.
Eben-ezer.note: Location: Often identified with Izbet Sartah.

definitions: Eber; the name of men in Scripture, especially the ancestor from whom the Hebrews are traditionally named.
Ebiasaph; a Korhite ancestor in the Levitical line, corresponding to the same name elsewhere written Abiasaph.
Ebony; a dark precious wood.
Ebronah; an encampment of Israel near the head of the Gulf of Aqaba during the wilderness journey.
Ebronah.note: Location: Near the northern end of the Gulf of Aqaba in the Arabah on Israel's southern wilderness route.

definitions: Ed; the memorial altar raised by the tribes beyond Jordan, meaning 'witness'.
Edar; a place-name associated with the tower of Eder near Bethlehem and also with a town in Judah's southern lists.
Edar.note: Location: Often identified with Khirbet es Siyar el Ghanam.

definitions: Eden; a name borne in Scripture by a person or house, distinct from the garden of Eden and the region so called.
Eder; the name of men in genealogical records, distinct from the place-name.
Edge; the sharp side of a blade, or the border or brink of a thing.
Plural form of 'edge'. See 'edge'.
To build up, especially in spiritual strength and understanding.
Building up spiritually; strengthening in faith and godliness.
Edom; the land and nation descended from Esau, and at times Esau himself.
The name often carries the idea of the neighboring people south of Judah.
Edom.note: Location: Usually located in or identified with Buseira.

definitions: Edomite; a member of the people of Edom, descended from Esau.
Edomites; the people descended from Esau, dwelling in Edom south of the Dead Sea and often standing in tense or hostile relation to Israel.
Edrei; a royal city of Bashan where Og was defeated.
Effect; result, operation, or working power.
Effected; brought about, accomplished, or made effective.
Powerful in producing the intended effect; working efficiently.
In a effectual manner or degree.
Effeminate; soft, unmanly, or morally corrupt in a way condemned by Scripture.
Egg; the egg of a bird or similar creature.
Plural form of 'egg'. See 'egg'.
Eglah; one of David's wives and the mother of Ithream.
Eglaim; a Moabite place named in prophetic lament over Moab's downfall.
Eglaim.note: Location: Often identified with Rujm el Jilimeh.

definitions: Eglon; king of Moab who oppressed Israel and was slain by Ehud.
Egypt; the land and kingdom so called, often standing for its people or power.
Egypt.note: Location: Usually located in or identified with Ain Shams.

definitions: Egyptian; a person of Egypt or something belonging to that land and its people.
Egyptians; the people of Egypt, often appearing as Israel's oppressors, neighbours, or allies depending on the passage.
Ehi; a Benjamite named in the tribal genealogies; the same figure is elsewhere given in the form Aharah.
Ehud; the judge who delivered Israel by slaying Eglon king of Moab.
The number eight.
The number eighteen.
Next after the seventeenth.
The one next after the seventh; occupying the eighth place.
Eightieth; older third-person singular form of 'eighty'. See 'eighty'.
The number eighty.
Either; one of two, or each of two.
Eker; a Benjamite ancestor in the genealogies.
Ekron; one of the five chief Philistine cities, notorious for the worship of Baal-zebub.
Ekron.note: Location: Commonly identified with Tel Miqne.

definitions: Ekronites; inhabitants of Ekron, one of the Philistine cities.
El-Beth-El; the altar-site at Bethel where God appeared to Jacob when he returned from Padan-aram.
El-beth-el.note: Location: Usually placed at or identified with in Beitin.

definitions: El-Elohe-Israel; a memorial or compound sacred name used in Scripture.
El-Paran; a place at the edge of the wilderness of Paran, named in the campaign of Chedorlaomer and his allies.
El-paran.note: Location: Often located in or identified with Aqaba, though the exact identification is debated.

definitions: Eladah; a descendant of Ephraim in the tribal genealogies.
Elah; the king of Israel slain by Zimri, and also the name of other biblical men.
Elam; the name of a man or family line in Scripture, distinct from the region of Elam.
Elamites; people of Elam named among those present at Pentecost and among the eastern nations.
Elamites.note: Location: Usually located in or identified with Shush.

definitions: Elasah; the name of men in Scripture, including one sent by king Zedekiah to Babylon.
Elath; the port or settlement on the Gulf of Aqaba used for trade and access to the Red Sea.
Elath.note: Location: Often located in or identified with Aqaba, though the exact identification is debated.

definitions: Eldaah; a son of Midian in the genealogies of Abraham's descendants.
Eldad; one of the elders upon whom the Spirit rested in the wilderness.
An elder; an older man, or one holding dignity, rule, and recognized standing among a people or in the church.
The term may refer to age, office, or representative authority.
Elders; older men or those holding rule and dignity in a people.
Oldest; first in age among children or others compared.
Elead; a descendant of Ephraim slain by the men of Gath.
Elealeh; a Moabite town often named with Heshbon in prophetic oracles.
Eleasah; the name of men in Scripture, including a descendant of Saul and one sent by Jeremiah with a letter to Babylon.
Eleazar; especially the son of Aaron, though the name is borne by others.
Chosen out; selected.
Often chosen by God to privilege, office, or saving mercy.
Elected; past tense or past participial form of 'elect'. See 'elect'.
the act of choosing, or the state of being chosen.
Plural form of 'element'. See 'element'.
Eleph; a Benjamite town named in the territorial lists near Jerusalem.
Eleph.note: Location: Often identified with Khirbet Salah.

definitions: The number eleven.
Next after the tenth.
Elhanan; the name of warriors in David's time, including the Bethlehemite noted in the giant traditions.
Eli; a man named in Scripture, father of Hophni.
Eliab; David's eldest brother, and also the name of other notable men of tribal and Levitical lines.
Eliada; one of David's sons, and also the name of a valiant captain under Jehoshaphat.
Eliadah; chiefly a son of David, though the name is also borne by other men such as one of David's mighty warriors.
Eliah; a Benjamite name in the genealogies.
Eliahba; one of David's mighty men.
Eliakim; son of Hilkiah in Hezekiah's court, and also the king of Judah later called Jehoiakim, with other men of the same name in Scripture.
Eliam; a mighty man of David, also associated with Bath-sheba's family.
Elias; the Greek name for Elijah the prophet.
Eliasaph; the name of tribal princes in the wilderness, including leaders of Gad and the Gershonites.
Eliashib; the high priest in the days of Nehemiah, and also the name of other biblical men.
Eliathah; a Levite or musician named in the chronicler's records.
Elidad; the son of Chislon and prince of Benjamin appointed in the division of the land.
Eliel; chiefly a name among David's mighty men and the Levites, and frequent in genealogical lists.
Elienai; the name of men in the genealogies, especially in the line of David and Benjamin.
Eliezer; Abraham's servant of Damascus and also the name of a son of Moses and other biblical men.
Elihoenai; a post-exilic or genealogical name, especially in the lines of David and Benjamin.
Elihoreph; one of Solomon's scribes.
Elihu; the young speaker in the book of Job, and also several other men of that name in genealogical records.
Elijah; the prophet of that name.
Elika; one of David's mighty men.
Elim; the oasis in the wilderness with springs and palm trees where Israel encamped after crossing the Red Sea.
Elim.note: Location: Often identified with Wadi Gharandal.

definitions: Elimelech; the husband of Naomi and father of Mahlon and Chilion in the book of Ruth.
Elioenai; chiefly a Davidic and post-exilic genealogical name, especially in the family records after the exile.
Eliphal; one of David's mighty men.
Eliphalet; the name of Davidic sons and other men in Scripture, elsewhere also written Eliphelet.
Eliphaz; the name of men in Scripture, especially Esau's son and Eliphaz the Temanite in the book of Job.
Elipheleh; a Levite or singer named in David's arrangements for the ark.
Eliphelet; chiefly a son of David, though the name is also borne by other men in the royal and military lists.
Elisabeth; the wife of Zacharias and mother of John the Baptist.
Eliseus; the Greek name for Elisha the prophet.
Elisha; son of Shaphat and successor of Elijah in the prophetic office.
Elishah; a son of Javan from whom maritime peoples or coasts were associated in early tradition.
Elishama; a prince of Ephraim in the wilderness, and also the name of other men in royal or tribal lines.
Elishaphat; one of the captains who supported Jehoiada in setting Joash on the throne.
Elisheba; the wife of Aaron and mother of his sons.
Elishua; one of David's sons born to him at Jerusalem.
Eliud; a name in the genealogy of Jesus recorded by Matthew.
Elizaphan; the name of men in Scripture, especially the son of Uzziel who was chief over the Kohathites.
Elizur; the son of Shedeur and prince of the tribe of Reuben in the wilderness.
Elkanah; the father of Samuel, and also the name of other biblical men.
Elkoshite; a person from Elkosh, used especially of Nahum the prophet.
Elkoshite.note: Location: Elkosh is uncertain; proposals range from Judah to Mesopotamia, so the precise homeland is not known.

definitions: Ellasar; the kingdom of Arioch named among the eastern confederate kings in Genesis.
Ellasar.note: Location: In Mesopotamia, often linked with southern Babylonian or Assyrian territory, though the exact identification is uncertain.

definitions: Elmodam; a name in the genealogy of Jesus recorded by Luke.
Plural form of 'elm'. See 'elm'.
Elnaam; the father of two of David's mighty men.
Elnathan; an officer of king Jehoiakim, and also the name of later post-exilic figures.
Eloi; an Aramaic form meaning my God, used by the Lord on the cross.
Elon; the name of men in Scripture, especially the judge of Israel from Zebulun and a Hittite father-in-law of Esau.
Elon-Beth-Hanan; a district or locality named in Solomon's administrative lists.
Elon-beth-hanan.note: Location: Often identified with Khirbet Wadi Illin.

definitions: Elonites; descendants of Elon reckoned among the families of Zebulun.
Eloquent; fluent, persuasive, or powerful in speech.
Eloth; the Red Sea port in the far south, also written Elath.
Eloth.note: Location: Often located in or identified with Aqaba, though the exact identification is debated.

definitions: Elpaal; a Benjamite ancestor in the genealogies.
Elpalet; a post-exilic descendant in the line of David, related in form to the name Eliphelet.
Else; besides, otherwise, or in addition.
Eltekeh; a Levitical town in Dan's territory, later involved in Assyrian-era conflict.
Eltekeh.note: Location: Often identified with Tel Shalaf.

definitions: Eltekon; a hill-country town of Judah named among its settlements.
Eltekon.note: Location: Often identified with Khirbet ed Deir.

definitions: Eltolad; a southern town allotted to Judah and later associated with Simeon.
Eltolad.note: Location: In the southern Negev near Beersheba.

definitions: Elul; the sixth month of the Hebrew civil year.
Eluzai; one of the warriors who came to David at Ziklag.
Elymas; the sorcerer and false prophet on Cyprus who withstood Paul before Sergius Paulus; Bar-jesus was another name for him.
Elzabad; the name of men in Scripture, including Benjamite and Gadite warriors who joined David.
Elzaphan; the son of Uzziel set over the Kohathites, also written Elizaphan.
Embalm; to prepare a dead body with spices or preservatives.
Embalmed; past tense or past participial form of 'embalm'. See 'embalm'.
Emboldened; past tense or past participial form of 'embolden'. See 'embolden'.
Emboldeneth; older third-person singular form of 'embolden'. See 'embolden'.
Embrace; to take in the arms, receive gladly, or accept.
Embraced; took in the arms, received gladly, or accepted.
Embracing; taking in the arms, receiving gladly, or accepting.
Embroider; to ornament cloth with needlework.
Embroiderer; one skilled in ornamental needlework.
Emerald; a precious green stone.
Plural form of 'emerald'. See 'emerald'.
Emerods; painful swellings or tumors in the Philistine plague narratives, likely severe boils or hemorrhoidal swellings.
Emims; an ancient giant-like people formerly dwelling in Moab.
Eminent; high, exalted, notable, or standing above others.
Emmanuel; a prophetic name meaning God with us, applied in the New Testament to Christ.
a village near Jerusalem visited by two disciples after the resurrection.
Emmaus.note: Location: Often identified with Qalunya, though the exact identification is debated.

definitions: Emmor; a man named in Scripture, father of Shechem.
Empire; rule or dominion over many lands or peoples.
Employ; to use, occupy, or engage in service.
Employed; past tense or past participial form of 'employ'. See 'employ'.
work, occupation, or use.
Emptied; made void, poured out, or deprived of contents.
Plural form of 'emptier'. See 'emptier'.
The state, quality, or condition of being emptines.
Empty; void, unfilled, or lacking substance or effect.
jealous rivalry or eager imitation.
Plural form of 'emulation'. See 'emulation'.
En-Eglaim; a place by the Dead Sea named in Ezekiel's vision of healing waters and fishermen.
En-Gannim; a Levitical town in Issachar and also a place-name in Judah under related usage.
En-gannim.note: Location: Often identified with Khirbet Umm Jina.

definitions: En-Gedi; the oasis stronghold by the Dead Sea where David hid from Saul.
En-gedi.note: Location: Usually located in or identified with Ein Gedi.

definitions: En-Haddah; a border town of Issachar in Joshua's territorial descriptions.
En-Hakkore; the spring or place opened for Samson after his great thirst.
En-hakkore.note: Location: At Lehi in the Shephelah of Judah, in the area of Samson's Philistine victory, though the exact spring is uncertain.

definitions: En-Hazor; a southern town of Judah named in the Negev settlements.
En-Mishpat; another name of Kadesh in the wilderness narrative.
En-mishpat.note: Location: Commonly identified with Ain el Qudeirat.

definitions: En-Rimmon; a southern town resettled after the exile, likely combining the names En and Rimmon.
En-rimmon.note: Location: Often identified with Tel Halif.

definitions: En-Rogel; a spring or fountain mentioned in Scripture.
En-rogel.note: Location: Commonly identified with Bir Ayub.

definitions: En-Shemesh; a spring or fountain mentioned in Scripture.
En-shemesh.note: Location: Commonly identified with Ein Haud.

definitions: En-Tappuah; a spring or fountain mentioned in Scripture.
En-tappuah.note: Location: In the hill country on the border of Ephraim and Manasseh, probably near the Tappuah district, though the exact spring is uncertain.

definitions: Enabled; past tense or past participial form of 'enable'. See 'enable'.
Enam; a place on the road where Tamar sat by the way in the Judah narrative.
Enam.note: Location: Often identified with Nahal Yarmut.

definitions: Enan; the father of Ahira of Naphtali in the wilderness order of the tribes.
To encamp is to pitch tents or take up a camp.
Encamped; past tense or past participial form of 'encamp'. See 'encamp'.
Encampeth; older third-person singular form of 'encamp'. See 'encamp'.
Encamping; present participial form of 'encamp', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'encamp'.
Enchanter; one who practices enchantments or magical arts.
Plural form of 'enchanter'. See 'enchanter'.
sorcery, incantation, or spell-like influence.
Plural form of 'enchantment'. See 'enchantment'.
Encountered; past tense or past participial form of 'encounter'. See 'encounter'.
to give heart, confidence, or courage to.
