definitions: Phichol; the chief captain of Abimelech in the days of Abraham and Isaac.
Philadelphia; the city addressed among the seven churches in Revelation.
It is especially commended for keeping Christ’s word with little strength.
Philadelphia.note: Location: In western Asia Minor, commonly identified with Alasehir in modern Turkey.

definitions: Philemon; the Christian master to whom Paul wrote concerning Onesimus.
Philetus; the false teacher named with Hymenaeus in the Pastoral Epistles.
Philip; chiefly Philip the apostle and Philip the evangelist, though the name belongs to more than one New Testament man.
Philippi; a principal city of Macedonia, notable for the first recorded gospel work in Europe.
It is especially associated with Paul, Silas, and the church addressed in the Epistle to the Philippians.
Philippi.note: Location: In eastern Macedonia, near modern Filippoi in northern Greece.

definitions: Philippians; inhabitants of Philippi, especially the Christian believers there addressed by Paul.
Philistia; the coastal land of the Philistines on the southwestern edge of Canaan.
Philistia.note: Location: Commonly identified with the Philistine plain around Ashdod, Gaza, Ashkelon, Ekron, and Gath.

definitions: Philistim; older form referring to the Philistines or their land.
Philistine; one of the Philistine people or something belonging to their coastal territory.
Philistine.note: Location: Usually located in or identified with Tel Ashkelon.

definitions: Philistines; the powerful coastal people who frequently opposed Israel in the days of the judges and kings.
Philologus; a Christian greeted by Paul in the Epistle to the Romans.
Plural form of 'philosopher'. See 'philosopher'.
Philosophy; a system of human reasoning or speculative wisdom, especially where contrasted with divine truth.
Phinehas; especially Aaron's grandson whose zeal stopped a plague in Israel, and also the name of other biblical men.
Phlegon; a Christian greeted by Paul in the Epistle to the Romans.
Phrygia; an inland region of Asia Minor named in connection with apostolic journeys.
The word chiefly marks territory and route rather than a single city.
Phrygia.note: Location: An inland region of west-central Asia Minor in what is now Turkey.

definitions: Phurah; the servant who went down with Gideon into the host of Midian.
Phut; a son of Ham in the table of nations, whose name also stands for the people descended from him.
Phuvah; a son of Issachar whose descendants formed a clan, related to the forms Pua and Puah.
Phygellus; one of those in Asia who turned away from Paul.
Plural form of 'phylactery'. See 'phylactery'.
Physician; one skilled in healing disease, a doctor or healer.
Plural form of 'physician'. See 'physician'.
Pi-beseth; an Egyptian city, usually identified with Bubastis.
Pi-beseth.note: Location: Usually identified with Tell Basteh.

definitions: Pi-Hahiroth; the encampment by the sea where Israel halted before the crossing in the Exodus.
Pi-hahiroth.note: Location: On the Exodus route by the sea before the crossing, traditionally placed on Egypt's northeastern side near the approaches to the Red Sea crossing.

definitions: Pick; a pointed tool for digging or breaking.
As a verb, to choose, pick out, or strike with a pick.
Plural form of 'picture'. See 'picture'.
Piece; a part, portion, fragment, or single article.
Pieces; parts, fragments, or individual items.
Pierce; to thrust through, penetrate, wound, or affect sharply.
Pierced; past tense or past participial form of 'pierce'. See 'pierce'.
Pierceth; older third-person singular form of 'pierce'. See 'pierce'.
Piercing; present participial form of 'pierce', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'pierce'.
Plural form of 'piercing'. See 'piercing'.
Piety; godliness, dutiful reverence, or devout conduct.
Pigeon; a dove-like bird, often linked with the sacrificial birds of Scripture.
Plural form of 'pigeon'. See 'pigeon'.
Pilate; Pontius Pilate, the Roman governor of Judaea who presided at the trial of Jesus and delivered him to be crucified.
Pildash; a son of Nahor in the patriarchal genealogies.
Pile; a heap, mass, or large structure.
As a verb, to heap up.
Pileha; one of the signers of Nehemiah's covenant.
the journey of a pilgrim, or life viewed as a sojourn.
Plural form of 'pilgrim'. See 'pilgrim'.
A pillar is a standing column or support.
In Scripture it may also signify permanence, memorial, support, or conspicuous strength.
Pillars; columns, posts, or principal supports.
Pilled; past tense or past participial form of 'pill'. See 'pill'.
Pillow; a cushion or support for the head.
Plural form of 'pillow'. See 'pillow'.
Plural form of 'pilot'. See 'pilot'.
Piltai; the head of the priestly house of Moadiah in the days of Joiakim.
Pin; a small pointed fastening piece or peg.
Pine; the tree so called.
As a verb, to waste away, mourn, or languish.
Pineth; older third-person singular form of 'pins'. See 'pins'.
Pining; present participial form of 'pin', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'pin'.
Pinnacle; a high projecting point or summit, especially of a building.
Pinon; an Edomite chief in the line of Esau.
Plural form of 'pin'. See 'pin'.
Pipe; a musical instrument of wind, tube, or hollow passage.
As a verb, to play on a pipe or utter with a shrill sound.
Piped; past tense or past participial form of 'pipe'. See 'pipe'.
Plural form of 'piper'. See 'piper'.
Plural form of 'pipe'. See 'pipe'.
Piram; king of Jarmuth who joined the confederacy against Gibeon.
Pirathon; the Ephraimite town of Abdon the judge.
Pirathon.note: Location: In the hill country of Ephraim southwest of Shechem, commonly identified with modern Fara'ta.

definitions: Pirathonite; an inhabitant, descendant, or member associated with Pirathon in Scripture.
Pisgah; the summit or ridge from which Moses viewed the promised land before his death.
Pisgah.note: Location: In the Moabite highlands east of the Dead Sea, commonly associated with the ridge of Nebo.

definitions: Pisidia; the inland region of Asia Minor so called in apostolic travel.
It is chiefly remembered in connection with Antioch in Pisidia and Paul’s journeys.
Pisidia.note: Location: An inland district of south-central Asia Minor, near Antioch in Pisidia.

definitions: Pison; one of the rivers of Eden in Genesis.
Pison.note: Location: Its exact course is unknown; proposed identifications place it in Arabia, Mesopotamia, or nearby regions associated with Havilah.

definitions: Pispah; a Benjamite ancestor in the genealogies.
to urinate.
Urinates.
Used in the older literal expression "him that pisseth against the wall."
Pit; a hole in the ground, cistern, trap, grave-like place, or figurative place of destruction.
Pitch; resinous substance, degree of height or tone, or a place set for a tent.
As a verb, to set or fix, especially a tent.
Set up, fixed, or spread out, especially of a tent or camp.
Pitcher; a vessel for drawing or carrying water or other liquid.
Plural form of 'pitcher'. See 'pitcher'.
Pithom; one of the store-cities built by the Israelites in Egypt for Pharaoh.
Pithom.note: Location: Often identified with Tell er Retaba.

definitions: Pithon; a son in the line of Benjamin and Saul.
Pitied; past tense or past participial form of 'pity'. See 'pity'.
Pitieth; older third-person singular form of 'pity'. See 'pity'.
Full of pity; compassionate and tenderhearted.
Not chiefly miserable in the modern sense.
Pits; holes, cisterns, snares, graves, or places of destruction.
Tender compassion toward one in misery or weakness.
Also the act of sparing or having mercy.
A place; a particular spot, position, office, or appointed room.
Placed; past tense or past participial form of 'place'. See 'place'.
Places; spots, positions, regions, or appointed locations.
A plague; a stroke, affliction, or severe calamity, whether upon a person or a people.
Plagued; past tense or past participial form of 'plague'. See 'plague'.
Plural form of 'plague'. See 'plague'.
Plain; clear, open, and easy to understand when used of speech or meaning.
As a noun, a plain is a broad level or gently open tract of land.
In a plain manner or degree.
Openness, simplicity, and straightforward clearness.
Plains; broad level or open tracts of land.
Plaister; plaster, a coating spread upon a wall or surface.
As a verb, to cover with plaster.
Plaistered; past tense or past participial form of 'plaister'. See 'plaister'.
Plaiting; present participial form of 'plait', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'plait'.
Plural form of 'plane'. See 'plane'.
Plural form of 'planet'. See 'planet'.
Plural form of 'plank'. See 'plank'.
A living thing that grows from the ground, or a shoot set out for growth.
To set in the ground, establish, or fix in place.
a planted place, colony, or the act of planting.
Planted; past tense or past participial form of 'plant'. See 'plant'.
Plantedst; means thou didst plant.
Plural form of 'planter'. See 'planter'.
Planteth; older third-person singular form of 'plant'. See 'plant'.
Planting; present participial form of 'plant', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'plant'.
Plural form of 'planting'. See 'planting'.
Plural form of 'plant'. See 'plant'.
Plat; a small plot or piece of ground, or a braid or fold.
Plate; a thin flat piece of metal, or a dish or vessel.
Plural form of 'plate'. See 'plate'.
Platted; braided, folded together, or arranged in a plat.
Platter; a broad dish for serving food.
Play; to sport, make pastime, perform on an instrument, or act lightly.
As a noun, sport or activity of play.
Played; sported, made pastime, performed on an instrument, or acted lightly.
Playedst; means thou didst play, perform, or act lightly.
Player; one who plays, especially a musician or performer.
Plural form of 'player'. See 'player'.
Playeth; means sports, performs, or acts lightly.
Playing; sporting, making pastime, performing, or acting lightly.
Plea; an earnest appeal, defense, or legal answer.
Plead; to argue a cause, make entreaty, contend in judgment, or intercede.
Pleaded; past tense or past participial form of 'plead'. See 'plead'.
Pleadeth; older third-person singular form of 'plead'. See 'plead'.
Plural form of 'pleading'. See 'pleading'.
Delightful, agreeable, or giving satisfaction.
The state, quality, or condition of being pleasantnes.
Pleased; gave satisfaction, found favor, or seemed good.
Pleases; gives satisfaction to; is agreeable to.
Pleasing; agreeable, acceptable, or giving satisfaction.
Pleasure; delight, satisfaction, desire, or will.
Pleasures; delights, satisfactions, desires, or expressions of will.
Pledge; a token, security, earnest, or thing given as assurance.
As a verb, to give in surety.
Plural form of 'pledge'. See 'pledge'.
Pleiades; a cluster of stars named in Job and Amos.
Abundant; full; richly supplied.
The state, quality, or condition of being plenteousnes.
Abundant, full, or richly supplied.
In a plentiful manner or degree.
Plenty; abundance, fullness, or large supply.
Plotteth; older third-person singular form of 'plot'. See 'plot'.
A plough is the instrument used to break and turn the ground; to plough is to till in that way.
The word is also used figuratively of labor that prepares for what is to follow.
Plow; the implement used to break the ground.
As a verb, to till with a plow.
Plowed; past tense or past participial form of 'plow'. See 'plow'.
Plural form of 'plower'. See 'plower'.

definitions: Rab-Mag; a Babylonian court title, likely denoting a chief official or high dignitary.

definitions: Rabboni; an emphatic Aramaic title akin to Rabbi, meaning my great master or teacher.

definitions: Rachel; the younger daughter of Laban and beloved wife of Jacob, mother of Joseph and Benjamin.

Ragau; the Greek name for Reu, an ancestor in the line from Shem to Abraham.
violent anger or fury; also to act with such force or passion.
Raged; past tense or past participial form of 'rage'. See 'rage'.
Rageth; older third-person singular form of 'rage'. See 'rage'.
Ragged; rough, torn, uneven, or clothed in rags.
Raging; present participial form of 'rage', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'rage'.
Plural form of 'rag'. See 'rag'.
Raguel; a man named in Scripture, father of Zipporah.
Rahab; the woman of Jericho who received the spies and was spared in Israel's conquest.
Raham; a Judahite name in the genealogies.
Rahel; the Greek name for Rachel.
Rail; to speak reproachfully, revile, or utter insults.
Railed; past tense or past participial form of 'rail'. See 'rail'.
Railer; one who reviles or speaks abusively.
Railing; present participial form of 'rail', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'rail'.
Plural form of 'railing'. See 'railing'.
Clothing; garments; apparel.
Water descending from the clouds.
As a verb, to pour down or cause to fall abundantly.
Rainbow; the bow seen in the cloud, set as a sign of God's covenant after the flood.
Rainy; full of rain or marked by rain.
Lifted up, stirred up, or caused to rise.
Raiser; one who raises up, lifts, or builds.
Raises; lifts up; causes to arise or stand.
It may also mean stirs up, appoints, or brings forth.
Raising; present participial form of 'raise', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'raise'.
Plural form of 'raisin'. See 'raisin'.
Rakem; the name of men in the genealogies, including a Midianite or tribal figure.
Rakkath; a fortified city of Naphtali, sometimes connected with the later site of Tiberias.
Rakkath.note: Location: On the western side of the Sea of Galilee in the territory of Naphtali.

definitions: Rakkon; a city in the territory of Dan, near the coast.
Rakkon.note: Location: Often identified with Tell er Reqqeit, though the exact identification is debated.

definitions: A ram; the male of the sheep kind, often used in sacrifice.
Rama; the Greek name for Ramah, the Benjaminite town associated with Samuel and cited in the New Testament.
Rama.note: Location: Commonly identified with Al Ram.

definitions: Ramah; chiefly the Benjaminite town associated with Samuel and with Rachel's lament, though several towns bear this name.
Ramah.note: Location: Several towns bear this name, most notably Ramah in Benjamin north of Jerusalem and Ramah associated with Samuel in the hill country; the passage determines which is meant.

definitions: Ramath; a height or town-name borne by more than one place in Scripture, especially in southern Judah and in Transjordan settings.
Ramath.note: Location: Often identified with Beer Yeroham.

definitions: Ramath-Lehi; the height or place named from Samson's use of the jawbone at Lehi.
Ramath-lehi.note: Location: At Lehi in the Shephelah of Judah, in the Samson narratives.

definitions: Ramath-Mizpeh; the Gadite border town in Gilead combining the ideas of height and watchtower.
Ramathaim-Zophim; the home of Elkanah and Samuel in the hill country of Ephraim.
Ramathaim-zophim.note: Location: In the hill country of Ephraim, traditionally linked with Ramah and often associated with the Ramallah area.

definitions: Ramathite; an inhabitant of Ramah or Ramathaim, depending on the passage.
Ramathite.note: Location: From one of the places called Ramah in the hill country of Benjamin or Ephraim, depending on context.

definitions: Rameses; a store city and district in Egypt associated with the Israelites' bondage and departure.
Rameses.note: Location: Often identified with Qantir, though the exact identification is debated.

definitions: Ramiah; one named in Ezra among the men who had taken strange wives.
Ramoth; a personal name in the genealogies, distinct from the place-name.
Ramoth-Gilead; the important Transjordan city of refuge and royal battleground in the territory of Gad.
Ramoth-gilead.note: Location: Commonly identified with Tall er Rumeith.

definitions: Rampart; a defensive wall, embankment, or fortified barrier.
Rams; male sheep, especially those used in sacrifice.
Ran; the past tense of run.
Rang; past tense of ring.
Range; a row, line, compass, or extent.
As a verb, to set in order or roam over.
Ranges; rows, ranks, extents, or cooking frames.
Ranging; present participial form of 'rang', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'rang'.
Rank; order, row, station, or degree.
As an adjective it may mean luxuriant, strong, or foul.
As a verb, to place in order.
Plural form of 'rank'. See 'rank'.
Ransom; the price of redemption or deliverance.
As a verb, to redeem by paying a price.
Ransomed; past tense or past participial form of 'ransom'. See 'ransom'.
Rapha; a Benjamite ancestor named in the genealogies, not to be confused with the Rephaim.
Raphu; the father of Palti, the spy from Benjamin.
Rare; uncommon or fine in quality, and in older usage sometimes thin or lightly made by context.
Rase; to level to the ground, raze, or scrape away.
Rash; hasty, unadvised, or reckless.
In a rash manner or degree.
Rate; a measure, reckoning, or assigned value.
As a verb, to estimate, value, or sometimes rebuke sharply.
Rather; more properly, more willingly, or to a greater degree according to the comparison in view.
Rattleth; older third-person singular form of 'rattle'. See 'rattle'.
Rattling; a rapid clattering sound, or the act of making such a sound.
Raven; the black bird of prey so called, counted among unclean birds.
Ravening; present participial form of 'raven', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'raven'.
Greedy for prey or food; fiercely devouring.
Plural form of 'raven'. See 'raven'.
Ravin; plunder, ravening, or violent seizure.
Ravished; past tense or past participial form of 'ravish'. See 'ravish'.
Raw; not cooked, unripe, or not fully worked or prepared.
Razor; a sharp instrument for shaving hair.
Reached; past tense or past participial form of 'reach'. See 'reach'.
Reaches; extends to; attains or comes as far as.
Reaching; present participial form of 'reach', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'reach'.
To read; to look upon and utter or understand what is written.
Readest; older second-person singular form of 'read', used with 'thou'. See 'read'.
Readeth; older third-person singular form of 'read'. See 'read'.
The state, quality, or condition of being readines.
Reading; present participial form of 'read', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'read'.
Ready; prepared, at hand, or willing.
Reaia; the name of men in Scripture, including a descendant of Shobal and one who returned from exile.
Reaiah; a name in the genealogies, also appearing among the returning families.
Realm; kingdom, dominion, or sphere of rule.
Reap; to gather a harvest, and figuratively to receive the fruit of what has been done.
Reaped; past tense or past participial form of 'reap'. See 'reap'.
A reaper is one who cuts and gathers the harvest.
In figurative use the word may point to agents of judgment or gathering at the end.
Plural form of 'reaper'. See 'reaper'.
Reapest; older second-person singular form of 'reap', used with 'thou'. See 'reap'.
Reapeth; older third-person singular form of 'reap'. See 'reap'.
Reaping; present participial form of 'reap', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'reap'.
Rear; the hinder part or back.
As a verb, to raise up, bring up, or build.
As an adjective, belonging to the back part.
Reared; past tense or past participial form of 'rear'. See 'rear'.
Reason; cause, argument, or the exercise of understanding.
Governed by reason; fitting or rational.
Reasoned; past tense or past participial form of 'reason'. See 'reason'.
Reasoning; present participial form of 'reason', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'reason'.
Plural form of 'reason'. See 'reason'.
Reba; one of the kings of Midian slain in the days of Moses.
Rebecca; a spelling of Rebekah, wife of Isaac and mother of Esau and Jacob.
Rebekah; a woman named in Scripture, daughter of Bethuel, wife of Isaac, mother of Esau.
Rebel; one who resists lawful authority.
As a verb, to resist, revolt, or turn in stubbornness.
Rebelled; past tense or past participial form of 'rebel'. See 'rebel'.
Rebellest; older second-person singular form of 'rebel', used with 'thou'. See 'rebel'.
Resistance against rightful rule; willful rising against authority.
Resisting lawful authority; stubbornly disobedient.
Plural form of 'rebel'. See 'rebel'.
To reprove sharply; to check by word of authority.
At times to restrain or suppress by command.
Rebuker; one who rebukes or reproves.
Plural form of 'rebuke'. See 'rebuke'.
Rebuking; present participial form of 'rebuke', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'rebuke'.
Recall; to call back, remember, or revoke.
As a noun, a calling back.
Receipt; receiving, revenue taken in, or the place where toll or tribute is collected.
To take, accept, or welcome what is given or brought.
Receivedst; means thou didst receive.
Receiver; one who receives, especially of payments or collections.
Receiving; present participial form of 'receive', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'receive'.
Rechab; the name of men in Scripture, especially the father of Jehonadab and a line remembered for loyalty.
Rechabites; the descendants or house of Rechab, remembered for their obedience to ancestral command.
Rechah; the father of a builder mentioned in Nehemiah's record.
Reckon; to count, account, consider, or impute.
Reckoned; past tense or past participial form of 'reckon'. See 'reckon'.
Reckoneth; older third-person singular form of 'reckon'. See 'reckon'.
Reckoning; present participial form of 'reckon', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'reckon'.
Recommended; past tense or past participial form of 'recommend'. See 'recommend'.
Repayment, return, or reward rendered for good or evil.
Plural form of 'recompence'. See 'recompence'.
Repayment, reward, or return made for good or evil.
Also to repay or requite.
Recompensest; older second-person singular form of 'recompense', used with 'thou'. See 'recompense'.
Recompensing; present participial form of 'recompense', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'recompense'.
To restore to favor, peace, or agreement after estrangement.
Reconciling; present participial form of 'reconcile', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'reconcile'.
Record; testimony, remembrance, written account, or witness.
As a verb, to bear witness or set down.
Recorded; past tense or past participial form of 'record'. See 'record'.
Recorder; an officer who keeps records or brings matters to remembrance before the king.
Plural form of 'record'. See 'record'.
Recount; to tell over, relate, or number up.
Recover; to regain, restore, rescue, or return to strength or possession.
Recovered; past tense or past participial form of 'recover'. See 'recover'.
Recovering; present participial form of 'recover', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'recover'.
Red; of a ruddy or blood-like color.
Reddish; having a somewhat red color.
To buy back, ransom, or deliver by payment or costly means.
Redeemedst; means thou didst redeem.
Redeemer; one who buys back, rescues, avenges as kinsman, or delivers; supremely the Lord in saving use.
Redeeming; present participial form of 'redeem', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'redeem'.
Deliverance secured by ransom; release obtained at a price.
The state, quality, or condition of being rednes.
Redound; to result, contribute, or turn to the advantage or effect of something.
Reed; a cane-like plant, and by extension a measuring reed or a staff made from such a plant.
Plural form of 'reed'. See 'reed'.
Reel; to stagger, sway, or whirl.
As a noun, a frame or thing that turns in winding.
Reelaiah; one of the leaders returning with Zerubbabel, corresponding to the same man elsewhere written Raamiah.
Refine; to purify by removing dross, and figuratively to make more pure.
Refined; past tense or past participial form of 'refine'. See 'refine'.
Refiner; one who purifies metal from dross, and figuratively one who purifies.
change for the better or correction of abuses.
Reformed; past tense or past participial form of 'reform'. See 'reform'.
Refrain; to hold back, cease, or keep from doing.
As a noun, a repeated saying or burden in song.
Refrained; past tense or past participial form of 'refrain'. See 'refrain'.
Refraineth; older third-person singular form of 'refrain'. See 'refrain'.
Refresh; to revive, restore strength, or bring relief.
Refreshed; past tense or past participial form of 'refresh'. See 'refresh'.
Refresheth; older third-person singular form of 'refresh'. See 'refresh'.
Refreshing; present participial form of 'refresh', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'refresh'.
A shelter, defense, or place of safety.
Refused; past tense or past participial form of 'refuse'. See 'refuse'.
Refusedst; means thou didst refuse.
Refuses; declines; will not accept or obey.
To look upon with consideration; to heed or respect.
Also esteem or favorable notice.
Regardest; older second-person singular form of 'regard', used with 'thou'. See 'regard'.
Regards; looks upon; esteems or takes notice of.
Regarding; present participial form of 'regard', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'regard'.
Regarding; concerning, about, or with respect to.
Regem; a son or descendant in the line of Caleb.
Regem-Melech; a man sent with others to inquire of the priests in the days of Zechariah.
New birth; renewal into new life.
At times also a great renewing or restoration.
A region; a district or tract of country marked off by position, rule, or common character.
The word often points to a surrounding area rather than a single town or city.
Plural form of 'region'. See 'region'.
Register; to record, enroll, or enter in a list.
As a noun, a written record or roll.
Rehabiah; a son of Eliezer in the line of Moses.
Rehearse; to recount, repeat, or set forth in order.
Rehearsed; past tense or past participial form of 'rehearse'. See 'rehearse'.
Rehob; a personal name in the genealogies and historical lists, distinct from the various places called Rehob.
Rehoboam; son of Solomon and first king of Judah after the kingdom was divided.
Rehoboth; the well Isaac named when the Lord gave room for him after disputes over the earlier wells.
Rehoboth is also the name of a city connected with Asshur in Genesis.
Rehoboth.note: Location: Often identified with Ruheibeh, though the exact identification is debated.

definitions: Rehum; chiefly a post-exilic official and Levitical name in Ezra and Nehemiah.
Rei; one of the loyal supporters of David at the time of Solomon's accession.
Ruled as king or sovereign.
Reignest; older second-person singular form of 'reign', used with 'thou'. See 'reign'.
Reigns; rules as king or sovereign; exercises prevailing dominion.
Reigning; present participial form of 'reign', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'reign'.
The inward parts, especially the kidneys, figuratively the seat of feeling or hidden affections.
Reject; to cast off, refuse, or set aside as not accepted.
Rejected; past tense or past participial form of 'reject'. See 'reject'.
Rejecteth; older third-person singular form of 'reject'. See 'reject'.
To be glad, exult, or take joy.
Rejoicest; older second-person singular form of 'rejoice', used with 'thou'. See 'rejoice'.
Joyful exultation; the act or state of rejoicing.
Rekem; the name of men in the genealogies, including a Midianite ruler and a Calebite line.
Release; to set free, let go, or discharge from obligation.
As a noun, freedom, remission, or discharge.
Released; past tense or past participial form of 'release'. See 'release'.
Relied; past tense or past participial form of 'rely'. See 'rely'.
Relief; aid, deliverance, or easing of distress.
Relieve; to ease, help, support, or bring deliverance from pressure.
Relieved; past tense or past participial form of 'relieve'. See 'relieve'.
Relieveth; older third-person singular form of 'relieve'. See 'relieve'.
Religion; outward worship, religious devotion, or the practice and profession of faith.
Devoted to religion, worship, or outward religious practice.
In a re manner or degree.
Remainder; what is left over, the residue, or remnant.
Remained; past tense or past participial form of 'remain'. See 'remain'.
Remainest; older second-person singular form of 'remain', used with 'thou'. See 'remain'.
Remains; continues; is left over after other things are removed or finished.
Remaining; present participial form of 'remain', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'remain'.
Remaliah; the father of Pekah king of Israel, often named in prophetic history to identify his son.
Remedy; relief, cure, or means of correcting an evil.
To call to mind, keep in mind, or act with mindful regard toward.
Called to mind; kept in remembrance; regarded with mindful attention.
Rememberest; older second-person singular form of 'remember', used with 'thou'. See 'remember'.
Remembering; present participial form of 'remember', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'remember'.
Memory; mindful recollection; a keeping in mind.
Also a memorial or token that calls something to mind.
Plural form of 'remembrance'. See 'remembrance'.
Remeth; a town in the inheritance of Issachar, commonly identified with the Jarmuth listed in another tribal boundary text.
Remeth.note: Location: In the territory of Issachar near the Jezreel plain, often linked with the region around Kokab el-Hawa.

definitions: Release, cancellation, or forgiveness, especially of sins.
Remit; to send back, relax, or forgive a debt, penalty, or sin.
Remitted; past tense or past participial form of 'remit'. See 'remit'.
Remmon; another biblical spelling of Rimmon, a southern Judahite or Simeonite town.
Remmon.note: Location: Often identified with Tel Halif.

definitions: Remmon-Methoar; a border point in Zebulun's inheritance, linked with the Rimmon locality.
A remnant; that which remains over, especially a surviving portion of a people.
Remove; to take away, put from its place, or in noun use a change of place or departure.
Removed; taken away, moved from place, or put out of office.
Removeth; older third-person singular form of 'remove'. See 'remove'.
Removing; present participial form of 'remove', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'remove'.
Remphan; the idolatrous star-god mentioned in Stephen's speech, linked with false worship carried by Israel.
Rend; to tear, split, or divide violently.
To give back, pay, or return what is due.
Renderest; older second-person singular form of 'render', used with 'thou'. See 'render'.
Rendereth; older third-person singular form of 'render'. See 'render'.
Rendering; present participial form of 'render', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'render'.
Rending; present participial form of 'rend', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'rend'.
To make fresh again; to restore or strengthen anew.
Renewest; older second-person singular form of 'renew', used with 'thou'. See 'renew'.
Renewing; present participial form of 'renew', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'renew'.
Renounced; past tense or past participial form of 'renounce'. See 'renounce'.
Renown; fame, honour, or notable reputation.
Renowned; past tense or past participial form of 'renown'. See 'renown'.
Rent; torn, split, or violently parted.
Rentest; older second-person singular form of 'rent', used with 'thou'. See 'rent'.
Repair; to restore, mend, or make whole again.
As a noun, condition or restoration.
Repaired; past tense or past participial form of 'repair'. See 'repair'.
Repairer; one who restores, mends, or rebuilds.
Repairing; present participial form of 'repair', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'repair'.
Repay; to pay back, requite, or return what is due for good or ill.
Repayed; past tense or past participial form of 'repay'. See 'repay'.
Repayeth; older third-person singular form of 'repay'. See 'repay'.
Repeateth; older third-person singular form of 'repeat'. See 'repeat'.
To turn in mind and purpose; to feel sorrow for sin and turn from it.
In some contexts it may speak more generally of relenting or changing course.
A change of mind and heart leading to turning from sin unto God.
Repentest; means thou repentest, turning in mind or sorrowing for sin and turning from it.
Repenting; present participial form of 'repent', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'repent'.
Plural form of 'repenting'. See 'repenting'.
Plural form of 'repetition'. See 'repetition'.
Rephael; a descendant in the line of Issachar or Benjamin in the genealogies.
Rephah; a descendant in the line of Ephraim.
Rephaiah; chiefly a name borne by builders, rulers, and descendants in Judah and Benjamin.
Rephaim; an ancient people of great stature mentioned in the Old Testament, sometimes associated with giants of earlier times.
Rephaims; a people of giant-like stature in early Old Testament tradition, and in some contexts the shades of the dead.
Rephidim; the wilderness encampment where water came from the rock and Amalek attacked Israel.
Rephidim.note: Location: Often identified with Wadi Rufaiyil, though the exact identification is debated.

definitions: Replenish; to fill, make full again, or furnish abundantly.
Replenished; past tense or past participial form of 'replenish'. See 'replenish'.
Repliest; older second-person singular form of 'reply', used with 'thou'. See 'reply'.
Report; tidings, rumor, account, or testimony brought back.
As a verb, to make known or bring word.
Reported; past tense or past participial form of 'report'. See 'report'.
Reproached; past tense or past participial form of 'reproach'. See 'reproach'.
Plural form of 'reproach'. See 'reproach'.
Reproachest; older second-person singular form of 'reproach', used with 'thou'. See 'reproach'.
Reproaches; brings shame upon; taunts or upbraids.
In a reproachful manner or degree.
Reprobate; rejected after testing, morally corrupt, or abandoned to wickedness.
As a noun, a wicked or castaway person.
Plural form of 'reprobate'. See 'reprobate'.
Correction, rebuke, or blame given for fault.
Plural form of 'reproof'. See 'reproof'.
Reprover; one who rebukes, corrects, or admonishes.
Reproveth; older third-person singular form of 'reprove'. See 'reprove'.
the character or standing by which one is known.
Reputed; regarded, accounted, or commonly esteemed.
A petition, desire, or thing asked for.
Also the act of making such a petition.
Requested; past tense or past participial form of 'request'. See 'request'.
To demand, ask for, or call for as due.
Requirest; older second-person singular form of 'require', used with 'thou'. See 'require'.
Requireth; older third-person singular form of 'require'. See 'require'.
Requiring; present participial form of 'require', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'require'.
Requite; to repay, reward, or return according to what has been done.
Requited; past tense or past participial form of 'requite'. See 'requite'.
Requiting; present participial form of 'requite', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'requite'.
The rereward is the rear guard or hindmost part of a marching company or host.
Rescue; to deliver from danger, violence, or captivity.
As a noun, such a deliverance.
Rescued; past tense or past participial form of 'rescue'. See 'rescue'.
Rescueth; older third-person singular form of 'rescue'. See 'rescue'.
likeness or similarity.
Resemble; to be like, bear likeness to, or have a similar appearance.
Resembled; past tense or past participial form of 'resemble'. See 'resemble'.
Resen; one of the early Assyrian cities named between Nineveh and Calah.
Resen.note: Location: Sometimes identified with Qaryat as Salamiyah.

definitions: Reserve; to keep back, retain, or hold for future use.
As a noun, what is kept back.
Reserved; past tense or past participial form of 'reserve'. See 'reserve'.
Reserveth; older third-person singular form of 'reserve'. See 'reserve'.
Resheph; a personal or poetic name in Scripture, distinct from its figurative use for flame or pestilence.
Residue; what remains over, the remnant, or the rest left after a part is taken.
to withstand, oppose, or strive against.
Resisted; past tense or past participial form of 'resist'. See 'resist'.
Resisteth; older third-person singular form of 'resist'. See 'resist'.
Resolved; past tense or past participial form of 'resolve'. See 'resolve'.
Resort; a place of gathering or recourse.
As a verb, to have recourse, go frequently, or turn to.
Resorted; past tense or past participial form of 'resort'. See 'resort'.
Respect; regard, consideration, or partial regard shown to persons.
As a verb, to regard or have reference to.
Respected; past tense or past participial form of 'respect'. See 'respect'.
Respecter; one who shows regard or partiality, especially of persons.
Respecteth; older third-person singular form of 'respect'. See 'respect'.
Respite; relief, pause, or temporary easing from trouble or judgment.
Relief from labor or disturbance; settled repose.
The word may also mean the remainder or what is left over.
Rested; past tense or past participial form of 'rest'. See 'rest'.
Restest; older second-person singular form of 'rest', used with 'thou'. See 'rest'.
Resteth; older third-person singular form of 'rest'. See 'rest'.
Resting; present participial form of 'rest', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'rest'.
Restingplace; a place of rest, settlement, or repose.
the restoring of what was lost, taken, or damaged.
Restore; to bring back, repay, reinstate, or return to a former state.
Restored; past tense or past participial form of 'restore'. See 'restore'.
Restorer; one who restores, repairs, or brings back.
Restoreth; older third-person singular form of 'restore'. See 'restore'.
Restrain; to hold back, check, or keep under control.
Restrained; past tense or past participial form of 'restrain'. See 'restrain'.
Restrainest; older second-person singular form of 'restrain', used with 'thou'. See 'restrain'.
Restraint; check, limitation, or holding back.
Plural form of 'rest'. See 'rest'.
Rising again from the dead; restoration to life.
Retain; to keep, hold fast, preserve, or continue to have.
Retained; past tense or past participial form of 'retain'. See 'retain'.
Retaineth; older third-person singular form of 'retain'. See 'retain'.
Retire; to withdraw, go back, or remove oneself.
Retired; past tense or past participial form of 'retire'. See 'retire'.
Came or went back again; turned back.
Returns; comes back; turns again.
Returning; present participial form of 'return', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'return'.
Reu; a patriarch in the line from Shem to Abraham.
Reuben; the son of Jacob and the tribe descended from him.
Reubenite; a member of the tribe of Reuben.
Reubenites; the members of the tribe descended from Reuben, especially settled east of Jordan.
Reuel; a Midianite elder associated with Jethro in Exodus, and also the name of other biblical men.
Reumah; the concubine of Nahor and mother of several sons.
to disclose or make known.
Revealed; past tense or past participial form of 'reveal'. See 'reveal'.
Revealer; one who makes known what was hidden.
Revealeth; older third-person singular form of 'reveal'. See 'reveal'.
disclosure, unveiling, or divine communication.
Plural form of 'revelation'. See 'revelation'.
Plural form of 'revelling'. See 'revelling'.
Revenge; the returning of injury for injury.
As a verb, to avenge or take vengeance.
Revenged; past tense or past participial form of 'revenge'. See 'revenge'.
Revenger; one who avenges, exacts satisfaction, or executes retribution.
Plural form of 'revenger'. See 'revenger'.
Plural form of 'revenge'. See 'revenge'.
Revengeth; older third-person singular form of 'revenge'. See 'revenge'.
Revenging; present participial form of 'revenge', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'revenge'.
Revenue; income, tribute, or return arising from property or taxation.
Plural form of 'revenue'. See 'revenue'.
deep respect or awe; also to regard with such honor.
Reverenced; past tense or past participial form of 'reverence'. See 'reverence'.
Reverend; worthy of reverence or awe.
Also used as an honorific title in later usage, though in Scripture chiefly an adjective.
Reverse; to turn back, overthrow, or make contrary.
As a noun, the opposite side or a contrary change.
To rail at, speak abusively against, or heap insulting words upon.
Plural form of 'reviler'. See 'reviler'.
Revilest; older second-person singular form of 'revile', used with 'thou'. See 'revile'.
Plural form of 'reviling'. See 'reviling'.
To bring back to life, strength, or vigor; to restore or quicken.
Revived; past tense or past participial form of 'revive'. See 'revive'.
Reviving; present participial form of 'revive', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'revive'.
Revolt; to rebel or turn away from allegiance.
As a noun, rebellion or defection.
Revolted; past tense or past participial form of 'revolt'. See 'revolt'.
Plural form of 'revolter'. See 'revolter'.
Revolting; present participial form of 'revolt', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'revolt'.
Recompense, return, or what is given in response to service or conduct.
Rewarder; one who gives reward or recompense.
Rewardeth; older third-person singular form of 'reward'. See 'reward'.
Plural form of 'reward'. See 'reward'.
Rezeph; a city cited in Assyrian boasts over conquered nations and lands.
Rezia; a descendant of Asher in the tribal genealogies.
Rezin; king of Syria who joined Pekah against Judah in the days of Ahaz.
Rezon; the Syrian adversary who became king in Damascus in the days of Solomon.
Rhegium; the Italian harbor at the toe of the peninsula where Paul's ship stopped on the voyage to Rome.
Rhegium.note: Location: Commonly identified with Reggio Calabria.

definitions: Rhesa; a name in the genealogy of Jesus recorded by Luke.
Rhoda; the maid who recognized Peter's voice at the gate in Acts.
Rhodes; the island and city of Rhodes in the eastern Mediterranean, mentioned in Acts as a stopping point on Paul's voyage.
Rhodes.note: Location: Commonly identified with Al Khuraybah.

definitions: Rib; one of the curved bones of the chest, or something shaped like one.
Ribai; the father of Ittai of Gibeah, one of David's mighty men.
Ribband; a band or strip used for binding or ornament; an older spelling of 'ribbon'.
Riblah; the Syrian city where Nebuchadnezzar established headquarters during the fall of Judah.
Plural form of 'rib'. See 'rib'.
Rich; having abundance of goods or resources, and also the rich as a class.
Richer; more rich in wealth, abundance, or quality.
Abundant wealth or valuable possession.
The word may also describe abundance of spiritual treasure.
In a rich manner or degree.
Rid; to deliver from, free from, or remove from one's presence or burden.
removal or deliverance from something troublesome.
Ridden; the past participle of ride, and by extension borne or oppressed by something.
Riddle; a dark saying, puzzling question, or enigmatic speech.
Rider; one who rides upon a beast, horse, or other conveyance.
Plural form of 'rider'. See 'rider'.
Rides; is carried along upon a beast or vehicle.
Plural form of 'ridge'. See 'ridge'.
Riding; present participial form of 'rid', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'rid'.
Rie; the grain rye; an older spelling of 'rye'.
Rifled; past tense or past participial form of 'rifle'. See 'rifle'.
Right; correct, just, straight, or on the right hand.
Just, upright, and in the right according to God's standard.
In a righteous manner or degree.
Justice, uprightness, and conformity to what is right before God.
Plural form of 'righteousness'. See 'righteousness'.
In a right manner or degree.
Rigour; severity, hardness, or stern strictness.
Rimmon; a personal name in some passages, distinct from the Syrian deity and the place-names.
Rimmon-Parez; an encampment of Israel in the wilderness itinerary.
Rimmon-parez.note: Location: On the southern wilderness route, probably in the northern Sinai or Negev.

definitions: Ring; a circular band, especially for the finger, or to sound, encircle, or resound.
Ringleader; the chief mover or leading person in a party or disturbance.
Plural form of 'ring'. See 'ring'.
Ringstraked; marked with ring-like streaks or bands.
Rinnah; a Judahite name in the genealogies.
Rinsed; past tense or past participial form of 'rinse'. See 'rinse'.
Wasteful, disorderly excess; loose and lavish living.
Rioting; present participial form of 'riot', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'riot'.
Rip; to tear open or divide by force.
As a noun, a tear or rent.
Ripe; fully grown, matured, fit for gathering, or ready in condition.
Ripening; present participial form of 'ripen', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'ripen'.
Riphath; a son of Gomer in the table of nations.
Ripped; past tense or past participial form of 'rip'. See 'rip'.
Risen; the past participle of rise, meaning gone up, stood up, or come forth.
Risest; older second-person singular form of 'rise', used with 'thou'. See 'rise'.
Rises; gets up; stands up; springs forth.
It may also mean arises in power, appears, or comes into action.
Rising; the act of ascending, standing up, or coming forth.
Rissah; a wilderness encampment in Israel's itinerary.
Rissah.note: Location: Often identified with Sharma, though the exact identification is debated.

definitions: Plural form of 'rite'. See 'rite'.
Rithmah; an encampment of Israel in the wilderness itinerary.
Rithmah.note: Location: On the southern wilderness route, often linked with the region of Wadi Ratamah in the northern Sinai or Negev.

definitions: A river; a considerable stream of flowing water.
It may denote either a named river or a major watercourse distinguished from smaller brooks.
Rivers; considerable streams of flowing water.
Rizpah; the daughter of Aiah and concubine of Saul who watched over the bodies of her sons.
Road; a way or highway for travel, and in some contexts a roadstead for ships.
Roar; to cry aloud with a loud deep sound, whether of beast, storm, or human lament.
As a noun, such a sound.
Roared; past tense or past participial form of 'roar'. See 'roar'.
Roareth; older third-person singular form of 'roar'. See 'roar'.
Roaring; present participial form of 'roar', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'roar'.
Plural form of 'roaring'. See 'roaring'.
Roast; to cook by fire or dry heat, or food so cooked.
Roasted; past tense or past participial form of 'roast'. See 'roast'.
Roasteth; older third-person singular form of 'roast'. See 'roast'.
Rob; to take away by force, fraud, or sacrilegious withholding.
Robbed; past tense or past participial form of 'rob'. See 'rob'.
Robber; one who takes by force or violence, especially by open theft.
Plural form of 'robber'. See 'robber'.
Robbery; violent or unlawful taking by force or fraud.
Robbeth; older third-person singular form of 'rob'. See 'rob'.
Robe; a long outer garment, often one of dignity, office, or state.
Plural form of 'robe'. See 'robe'.
Roboam; the Greek name for Rehoboam, first king of Judah after the division.
A rock; a mass of stone, and figuratively a place of strength or refuge.
Plural form of 'rock'. See 'rock'.
Rod; a staff, switch, scepter, measuring rod, or instrument of correction and authority.
Rode; the past tense of ride.
Rods; staffs, scepters, instruments of correction, or signs of authority.
Roe; a roe or roebuck, a swift and graceful wild deer.
Roebuck; a small deer or gazelle-like animal so named in Scripture.
Plural form of 'roebuck'. See 'roebuck'.
Plural form of 'roe'. See 'roe'.
Rogelim; the Transjordan home of Barzillai's family in the days of David.
Rohgah; a descendant of Asher in the tribal genealogies.
Roll; a scroll, a turning motion, or a rounded mass.
As a verb, to turn over, wrap, or move by turning.
Rolled; past tense or past participial form of 'roll'. See 'roll'.
Roller; that which rolls, or a rolling implement.
Rolleth; older third-person singular form of 'rolls'. See 'rolls'.
Rolling; present participial form of 'roll', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'roll'.
Plural form of 'roll'. See 'roll'.
Romamti-Ezer; a son of Heman named among the temple musicians.
Roman; a person of Rome or something belonging to Roman citizenship, law, or power.
Romans; the people of Rome or those belonging to Roman rule and citizenship.
Rome; the capital city of the empire under which much of the New Testament history unfolds.
The name may also suggest imperial authority, citizenship, or power.
Rome.note: Location: In central Italy, the imperial capital of the Roman world in New Testament times.

definitions: A roof is the upper covering of a house or structure.
In Scripture the housetop often serves as a place of prayer, proclamation, retreat, or exposure.
Plural form of 'roof'. See 'roof'.
Place; space; opportunity.
Not necessarily an enclosed chamber.
Plural form of 'room'. See 'room'.
Root; the underground part of a plant, and figuratively origin, source, or fixed cause.
Rooted; past tense or past participial form of 'root'. See 'root'.
Plural form of 'root'. See 'root'.
A rope is a thick cord or line for binding, drawing, or fastening.
Plural form of 'rope'. See 'rope'.
Rose; the past tense of rise, meaning went up, stood up, or increased.
Rosh; a son of Benjamin named in the tribal genealogies.
Rot; to decay, corrupt, or become putrid.
As a noun, decay or corruption.
Rotten; decayed, corrupt, unsound, or spoiled.
The state, quality, or condition of being rottennes.
Rough; uneven, harsh, severe, or unpolished.
In a rough manner or degree.
Round; around on every side; in a circle or circuit.
Rouse; to stir up, awaken, or excite into action.
Plural form of 'rover'. See 'rover'.
Row; a line, rank, or orderly series set one after another.
Rowed; past tense or past participial form of 'row'. See 'row'.
Plural form of 'rower'. See 'rower'.
Rowing; present participial form of 'row', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'row'.
Plural form of 'row'. See 'row'.
Kingly; belonging to a king or kingdom; regal.
Ru-Hamah; a symbolic name meaning having obtained mercy or shown mercy.
Rubbing; the act of rubbing, scraping, or chafing.
Rubbish; ruins, broken fragments, or worthless refuse.
Plural form of 'ruby'. See 'ruby'.
Rudder; the steering blade or instrument of a ship, used figuratively for guidance.
Ruddy; reddish in colour, especially healthy or fresh in complexion.
Untrained, unskilled, or plain rather than polished.
Not necessarily insolent in the modern sense.
Plural form of 'rudiment'. See 'rudiment'.
Rue; an herb named among the garden plants.
Rufus; a Christian known in the New Testament, possibly the son of Simon of Cyrene and one greeted by Paul.
Ruin; destruction, collapse, or decay.
As a verb, to destroy or bring to ruin.
Ruined; past tense or past participial form of 'ruin'. See 'ruin'.
Bringing ruin, decay, or destruction.
Plural form of 'ruin'. See 'ruin'.
Ruled; past tense or past participial form of 'rule'. See 'rule'.
A ruler; one who governs or exercises authority.
The word may apply to a magistrate, prince, chief, or other person set over others.
Rulers; those who govern or exercise authority.
The term may refer to civil, tribal, religious, or royal authorities.
Rulest; older second-person singular form of 'rule', used with 'thou'. See 'rule'.
Rules; governs; exercises authority over.
Ruling; present participial form of 'rule', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'rule'.
Rumah; the town of Zebudah, mother of King Jehoiakim.
Rumah.note: Location: Sometimes identified with Horbat Rumah.

definitions: Rumbling; a deep rolling sound, or the act of making such a sound.
Rumour; report, tidings, or common talk spread abroad.
Plural form of 'rumour'. See 'rumour'.
Rump; the hind part or backside of an animal.
Runnest; older second-person singular form of 'run', used with 'thou'. See 'run'.
Runs; moves swiftly; hastens.
It may also be said of water or of the course of a thing.
Running; present participial form of 'run', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'run'.
Rush; a reed-like plant of wet places.
As a verb, to move or drive with sudden force and haste.
Rushed; past tense or past participial form of 'rush'. See 'rush'.
Plural form of 'rush'. See 'rush'.
Rusheth; older third-person singular form of 'rush'. See 'rush'.
Rushing; present participial form of 'rush', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'rush'.
Rust; the reddish corrosion of metal, or to become so corroded.
Ruth; the Moabitess who clave to Naomi, married Boaz, and entered the line of David and of Christ.
S; the letter s.
Sabachthani; Aramaic for hast forsaken me, preserved in the cry of Jesus from the cross.
Sabaoth; hosts or armies, preserved in the divine title Lord of Sabaoth, meaning Lord of hosts.
A day of sacred rest set apart unto God.
By use it may speak of the weekly holy day or of other solemn rest-days appointed under the law.
Plural form of 'sabean'. See 'sabean'.
Sabta; a son of Cush in the table of nations, also written Sabtah.
Sabtah; a son of Cush in the table of nations, also written Sabta.
Sabtecha; a son of Cush in the table of nations, also written Sabtechah.
Sacar; father of Ahiam, one of David's mighty men, corresponding to the same person elsewhere written Sharar.
Sack; a bag or coarse cloth, and by extension sackcloth worn in mourning.
Sackbut; one of the instruments named in the Babylonian orchestra, an ancient musical instrument of uncertain exact modern equivalent.
A coarse cloth, often of hair, worn in mourning, humiliation, or penitence.
Plural form of 'sackcloth'. See 'sackcloth'.
Plural form of 'sack'. See 'sack'.
A sacrifice; an offering presented to God, commonly by slaying or yielding up something dedicated.
The word may also extend to spiritual self-offering, praise, or costly devotion.
Sacrificed; past tense or past participial form of 'sacrifice'. See 'sacrifice'.
Sacrificedst; means thou didst sacrifice or offer up.
Sacrifices; offerings presented to God, especially those made by slaying and offering victims.
The word may also include spiritual offerings by extension.
Sacrificeth; older third-person singular form of 'sacrifice'. See 'sacrifice'.
Sacrificing; present participial form of 'sacrifice', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'sacrifice'.
Sacrilege; profanation of what is holy, robbery or misuse of sacred things.
Sad; sorrowful, grave, or heavy in countenance or spirit.
Saddle; the seat or gear laid on a beast for riding.
As a verb, to equip with a saddle or lay a burden upon.
Saddled; past tense or past participial form of 'saddle'. See 'saddle'.
Sadducees; the Jewish sect known in the New Testament for denying the resurrection and opposing Jesus and the apostles in controversy.
In a sad manner or degree.
The state, quality, or condition of being sadnes.
Sadoc; a Greek name-form of Zadok appearing in the genealogy of Jesus in Matthew.
Safe; secure, preserved from harm, or unhurt.
Safeguard; protection or defense.
As a verb, to guard safely.
Safely; in a safe manner, without harm or danger.
Safety; preservation from danger, security, or deliverance.
Saffron; a fragrant plant or spice named among precious growths.
Said; spoke; declared; told.
Saidst; means thou saidst or spoke.
Sail; the canvas of a ship or the voyage made by it.
As a verb, to travel by ship.
Sailed; past tense or past participial form of 'sail'. See 'sail'.
Sailing; present participial form of 'sail', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'sail'.
Plural form of 'sailor'. See 'sailor'.
Saint; a holy one, especially one belonging to God and set apart to him.
Saints; holy ones, especially the people of God set apart to him.
Says; speaks; declares.
Sake; cause, reason, interest, or regard.
Plural form of 'sake'. See 'sake'.
Sala; the Greek name for Salah or Shelah in the genealogical line after the flood.
Salah; a patriarch in the line from Shem, rendered Sala in the Greek form.
a city on Cyprus where Paul first preached in the synagogues.
Salathiel; the Greek name for Shealtiel.
Salcah; the eastern frontier city of Bashan in the territory later held by Manasseh.
Salcah.note: Location: In Bashan at the southeastern edge of the Hauran, commonly identified with modern Salkhad.

definitions: Salchah; the eastern Bashan city marking the edge of Israel's Transjordan possession.
Sale; the act of selling, or what is sold for a price.
Salem; the name associated with Melchizedek's royal city, commonly understood as an early name of Jerusalem.
Salem is also used of a place near Aenon in the Gospel account.
Salim; the place near Aenon where John was baptizing because there was much water there.
Salim.note: Location: Sometimes identified with Tel Shalem.

definitions: Sallai; the name of post-exilic Benjamites and officials dwelling at Jerusalem.
Sallu; chiefly a post-exilic Benjamite and gatekeeping name among those dwelling at Jerusalem.
Salma; a genealogical name-form of Salmon in the line of Judah.
Salmon; an ancestor in the line of Judah and of David, husband of Rahab according to the genealogical tradition of Matthew.
Salmone; a headland or projecting height mentioned in Scripture.
Salmone.note: Location: Usually placed at or identified with Cape Sideros.

definitions: Salome; the name of women in the New Testament, especially the follower of Jesus who came to the sepulchre and the daughter of Herodias in later tradition.
Salt; the mineral used for seasoning and preserving, and figuratively what purifies, seasons, or preserves.
Salted; past tense or past participial form of 'salt'. See 'salt'.
The state, quality, or condition of being saltnes.
Plural form of 'saltpit'. See 'saltpit'.
Salu; the father of Zimri, the Simeonite prince slain at Baal-peor.
A greeting; words of welcome or respectful address.
Salute; to greet, address with respect, or acknowledge with peace or honor.
As a noun, a greeting.
Saluted; past tense or past participial form of 'salute'. See 'salute'.
Saluteth; older third-person singular form of 'salute'. See 'salute'.
Deliverance, preservation, and rescue, especially from sin and judgment.
Samaria; the capital city of the northern kingdom and, by extension, the surrounding region.
Samaria.note: Location: In the central hill country between Judaea and Galilee; sometimes the city, sometimes the wider district.

definitions: Samaritan; one belonging to Samaria or to the mixed people and worship centered there, in contrast with the Jews.
Samaritans; the people of Samaria, distinguished from the Jews by ancestry, worship, and long-standing tension.
Identical; not different.
Samgar-Nebo; a Babylonian ruler or officer named among those present at the fall of Jerusalem.
Samlah; an Edomite king who reigned after Hadad.
an island in the Aegean Sea near the coast of Asia Minor.
Samothracia; the island called at in Paul's voyage across the Aegean.
Samothracia.note: Location: Usually located in or identified with Samothraki.

definitions: Samson; the Nazarite judge of Israel famed for extraordinary strength and for his conflict with the Philistines.
Samuel; the prophet and judge of that name.
Sanballat; the adversary who opposed Nehemiah and the rebuilding of Jerusalem's wall.
Made holy; set apart unto sacred use or cleansed for God.
Sanctifies; sets apart as holy; consecrates or makes holy.
To set apart as holy; to consecrate.
Also to cleanse and make holy in life and use.
A sanctuary; a holy place set apart for the worship or presence of God.
It may refer to the whole sacred precinct, the inner holy place, or a place of refuge.
Sand; fine loose particles of earth, often used to express multitude or littleness.
Plural form of 'sandal'. See 'sandal'.
Sang; the past tense of sing.
Sank; past tense of sink.
Sansannah; a southern town of Judah in the Negev, likely related to Hazar-Susah in parallel lists.
Sansannah.note: Location: Often identified with Khirbet esh Shamsaniyat.

definitions: Sap; the vital juice of plants and trees.
Saph; a Philistine giant slain by Sibbecai in David's wars; the same giant is given under the related form Sippai.
Saphir; a Judean town named in Micah's lament over the coming invasion.
Saphir.note: Location: Sometimes identified with Tell as Sawafir.

definitions: Sapphira; the wife of Ananias who lied to the Holy Ghost and fell dead in Acts.
Sapphire; the precious stone so called, used in descriptions of beauty and glory.
Plural form of 'sapphire'. See 'sapphire'.
Sara; the Greek name for Sarah.
Sarah; the wife of Abraham and mother of Isaac, first called Sarai.
Sarai; the earlier name of Sarah, wife of Abram before the covenant name-change.
Saraph; a Judahite name in the genealogies.
Sardine; a precious stone, commonly linked with sardius or similar red gem terminology in older usage.
Sardis; the city addressed among the seven churches in Revelation.
It is remembered there for having a name to live while needing spiritual awakening.
Sardis.note: Location: In western Asia Minor, commonly identified with Sardis near modern Sart in Turkey.

definitions: Sardites; descendants of Sered reckoned among the families of Zebulun.
a reddish precious stone; sard.
Sardonyx; a precious stone of layered reddish and whitish appearance.
Sarepta; the Greek name for Zarephath, the Phoenician town where Elijah stayed with the widow.
Sargon; a man named in Scripture, father of Adrammelech.
Sarid; a border town of Zebulun named in the tribal allotment.
Sarid.note: Location: Commonly identified with Tel Shadud.

definitions: Saron; the fertile coastal plain better known under the form Sharon.
Saron.note: Location: Usually located in or identified with Sharon Plain.

definitions: Sarsechim; a Babylonian officer present at the fall of Jerusalem.
Saruch; the Greek name for Serug, an ancestor in the line from Shem to Abraham.
The past tense of sit; was seated, remained, or dwelt in a place.
Satan; the adversary, the chief evil spirit who opposes God and tempts or accuses men.
Satest; means thou satest, that is, thou didst sit, remain seated, or dwell.
Satiate; to fill fully, satisfy, or glut.
Satiated; past tense or past participial form of 'satiate'. See 'satiate'.
contentment, fulfillment, or amends made.
Satisfiest; older second-person singular form of 'satisfy', used with 'thou'. See 'satisfy'.
Satisfieth; older third-person singular form of 'satisfy'. See 'satisfy'.
To fill, content, or supply fully.
Satisfying; present participial form of 'satisfy', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'satisfy'.
Satyr; a wild creature or goat-like being named in prophetic descriptions of desolate places.
Plural form of 'satyr'. See 'satyr'.
Saul; especially the first king of Israel, though the name is also borne by others.
Keep alive; preserve from death.
Delivered, preserved, or rescued from danger, judgment, or ruin.
Savest; older second-person singular form of 'save', used with 'thou'. See 'save'.
Saves; delivers, preserves, or rescues from danger or ruin.
Saving; present participial form of 'save', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'save'.
Saviour; one who saves, delivers, or preserves; supremely God or Christ in redemptive use.
Plural form of 'saviour'. See 'saviour'.
Taste, smell, or perceived quality.
Figuratively, inward relish, bent, or judgment.
Plural form of 'savour'. See 'savour'.
Savoury; pleasant to the taste or smell, especially well-seasoned and agreeable as food.
The past tense of see.
Sawed; past tense or past participial form of 'saw'. See 'saw'.
Sawest; means thou sawest or beheldest.
Sawn; cut with a saw, or the past participle of saw.
Plural form of 'saw'. See 'saw'.
Sayest; thou sayest; you say or speak.
A thing spoken; a statement, proverb, or report.
Scab; a crusted eruption or sore, especially in legal descriptions of uncleanness.
Scabbard; the sheath of a sword or dagger.
Scabbed; past tense or past participial form of 'scab'. See 'scab'.
Scaffold; a raised platform or staging.
Plural form of 'scale'. See 'scale'.
Scaleth; means climbs, ascends, or forms scales.
Scall; a scurfy or scabby eruption named in the law of leprosy.
Scalp; the skin or upper covering of the head.
Barely sufficient; meager or limited.
Scapegoat; the goat sent away on the Day of Atonement bearing away the confessed sins of the people, and figuratively one made to bear blame for others.
Hardly; barely; with difficulty.
Also rare or not plentiful in some contexts.
In a scarce manner or degree.
The state, quality, or condition of being scarcenes.
Scarest; older second-person singular form of 'scare', used with 'thou'. See 'scare'.
Scarlet; a bright red color, scarlet cloth, or something marked by that color.
Dispersed, spread abroad, or driven apart.
Scatters; disperses; causes to be spread abroad.
Scattering; present participial form of 'scatter', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'scatter'.
Smell, fragrance, or odor.
To perceive by smell or to track by odor.
Sceptre; the staff or rod of kingly rule and authority.
Plural form of 'sceptre'. See 'sceptre'.
Sceva; the Jewish chief priest whose sons attempted to use the name of Jesus in Ephesus.
division or separation within a body.
Scholar; a learner, student, or one instructed by a teacher.
School; a company or setting of instruction and learning.
Schoolmaster; a teacher, and in Paul's figurative use a disciplinarian or tutor leading unto Christ.
knowledge or disciplined understanding.
Scoff; to mock with contempt.
As a noun, mocking speech.
Plural form of 'scoffer'. See 'scoffer'.
Scorch; to burn superficially, parch, or wither with heat.
Scorched; past tense or past participial form of 'scorch'. See 'scorch'.
To despise with mockery or proud contempt.
Also the expression of such contempt.
A mocker; one who treats truth or correction with proud contempt.
Scornest; older second-person singular form of 'scorn', used with 'thou'. See 'scorn'.
Scorneth; older third-person singular form of 'scorn'. See 'scorn'.
Full of scorn; contemptuous.
Scorpion; a venomous creature with a stinging tail, also used figuratively for cruel scourges or torment.
Plural form of 'scorpion'. See 'scorpion'.
Scoured; past tense or past participial form of 'scour'. See 'scour'.
Scourge; a whip or instrument of punishment, and figuratively a severe judgment or affliction.
As a verb, to whip or chastise.
Scourged; past tense or past participial form of 'scourge'. See 'scourge'.
Plural form of 'scourge'. See 'scourge'.
Scourgeth; older third-person singular form of 'scourge'. See 'scourge'.
Scourging; present participial form of 'scourge', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'scourge'.
Plural form of 'scourging'. See 'scourging'.
Scrabbled; past tense or past participial form of 'scrabble'. See 'scrabble'.
Scrape; to rub or draw over a surface so as to remove or gather something.
As a noun, a scratch or awkward difficulty.
Scraped; past tense or past participial form of 'scrape'. See 'scrape'.
Screech; to utter a harsh shrill cry.
As a noun, such a cry.
A scribe; a writer, copyist, or learned expounder of the law.
In the New Testament the term often refers to one skilled in the Scriptures and associated with teaching or legal interpretation.
Scribes; learned writers, copyists, or teachers of the law.
A small bag, wallet, or satchel for carrying provisions.
A traveler's bag and walking staff.
sacred writing; especially the inspired writings of the Bible.
Plural form of 'scripture'. See 'scripture'.
Scroll; a rolled writing or book-form document.
Scum; foul froth, refuse, or impurity gathered on the surface.
Scurvy; vile, contemptible, or base.
Scythian; a member of the Scythian people, used by Paul as an example of a foreign people regarded by the world as especially barbarous.
The sea; a large body of water.
The word may mean the open sea, a great inland water, or a named body of water.
Seafaring; present participial form of 'seafaring', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'seafaring'.
a stamp or mark used to secure or authenticate; also to fasten or confirm with such a seal.
Sealed; past tense or past participial form of 'seal'. See 'seal'.
Sealest; older second-person singular form of 'seal', used with 'thou'. See 'seal'.
Sealeth; older third-person singular form of 'seal'. See 'seal'.
Sealing; present participial form of 'seal', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'seal'.
Plural form of 'seal'. See 'seal'.
Seam; the line where things are joined by sewing or fitting together.
To examine carefully, seek out, or inquire diligently.
Searchest; means thou searchest, examining carefully, seeking out, or inquiring diligently.
Searching; present participial form of 'search', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'search'.
Plural form of 'searching'. See 'searching'.
Seared; past tense or past participial form of 'sear'. See 'sear'.
Seas; large bodies of water, whether open seas or great inland waters.
Season; a fitting time, appointed opportunity, or period of the year.
Seasoned; made suitable, flavored, matured, or adapted to a time.
Seasons; fitting times, appointed opportunities, or periods of the year.
Seat; a place to sit, a settled position, or a seat of rule or authority.
Seated; set in place, made to sit, or established in position.
Seats; places to sit, settled positions, or places of rule.
Seatward; toward the seat or place of sitting.
Seba; a son of Cush and ancestor of a people or kingdom associated with wealth and distance.
Sebat; the eleventh month of the post-exilic Hebrew year.
Secacah; a town in the wilderness of Judah near the Dead Sea district.
Secacah.note: Location: Often identified with Khirbet es Samrah.

definitions: Sechu; the great well or locality where Saul halted on his way toward Ramah.
Sechu.note: Location: Commonly identified with the area between Gibeah and Ramah.

definitions: Next after the first.
In a secondari manner or degree.
Secret; a hidden matter, or something hidden and private.
In a secret manner or degree.
Secrets; hidden matters or private things.
Sect; a party, division, or body distinguished by shared opinions or practices.
Secundus; the Thessalonian believer who accompanied Paul.
safe or free from danger or care; also to make safe or obtain firmly.
In a secure manner or degree.
safety, assurance, or something pledged as guarantee.
rebellion or incitement against lawful authority.
Plural form of 'sedition'. See 'sedition'.
Seduce; to lead astray, entice into error, or draw away from what is right.
Seduced; past tense or past participial form of 'seduce'. See 'seduce'.
Plural form of 'seducer'. See 'seducer'.
Seduceth; older third-person singular form of 'seduce'. See 'seduce'.
Seducing; present participial form of 'seduce', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'seduce'.
Seed; that which is sown, offspring, descendants, or posterity.
Seeds; that which is sown, or descendants and posterity in the plural.
Seedtime; the season for sowing seed.
Seeing; the act of beholding, and also since or inasmuch as.
Seekest; means thou seekest, lookest for, or pursuest after.
Seeks; looks for; pursues after.
Often implies earnest desire, inquiry, or effort to obtain.
Seeking; present participial form of 'seek', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'seek'.
Seemed; past tense or past participial form of 'seem'. See 'seem'.
Seems; appears; is judged or thought to be so.
Becoming, decent, fitting, or proper.
The past participle of see; beheld or perceived.
Seer; an older title for a prophet, one who sees by divine revelation.
Plural form of 'seer'. See 'seer'.
Seest; thou seest; you see or perceive.
Often carries the further sense of understanding or taking notice.
Sees; beholds; perceives.
Often includes understanding, taking notice, or discerning.
Seethe; to boil, sodden, or cook in liquid.
Seething; present participial form of 'seethe', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'seethe'.
Segub; the name of men in Scripture, including a son of Hiel and a descendant of Judah.
Seir; the mountainous region associated with Edom and the descendants of Esau.
The name may refer to the land, the mountain range, or the people dwelling there.
Seir.note: Location: Commonly located in or identified with Jebel esh Shera.

definitions: Seirath; the wooded or hilly district to which Ehud escaped after slaying Eglon.
Seirath.note: Location: Often located in or identified with hills of Ephraim, though the exact identification is debated.

definitions: Seize; to take hold of suddenly, lay hold on by force, or grasp.
Seized; past tense or past participial form of 'seize'. See 'seize'.
Sela; the Edomite stronghold captured in the days of Amaziah, often identified with the rock city later called Petra.
Sela-Hammahlekoth; the rock of divisions, the place where Saul broke off his pursuit of David because of a Philistine invasion.
Sela-hammahlekoth.note: Location: Often located in or identified with Wadi Malaqi, though the exact identification is debated.

definitions: Selah; a musical or liturgical term in the Psalms, likely marking a pause or lifting up.
Seled; a descendant of Jerahmeel in the genealogies of Judah.
Seleucia; the port of Syrian Antioch from which Paul and Barnabas sailed on the first missionary journey.
Seleucia.note: Location: Commonly identified with Seleucia Pieria.

definitions: Self; one's own person, nature, or being.
Used with pronouns for emphasis, as in himself or themselves.
Selfsame; the very same, identical, or that very one.
Selfwill; obstinacy of one's own will, wilfulness.
Selfwilled; past tense or past participial form of 'selfwill'. See 'selfwill'.
Sell; to give over for a price, to betray for gain, or to deliver into another's power.
Seller; one who sells or offers goods for sale.
Plural form of 'seller'. See 'seller'.
Sellest; means thou sellest, giving over for a price, betraying for gain, or delivering into another's power.
Selleth; older third-person singular form of 'sell'. See 'sell'.
Selvedge; the finished edge of woven cloth or curtain work.
Plural form of 'self'. See 'self'.
Sem; a man named in Scripture, son of Noah, father of Asshur.
Semachiah; one of the sons of Shemaiah among the gatekeepers.
Semei; a name-form of Shimei or Simeon appearing in genealogical and New Testament lists.
Senaah; a place whose descendants returned from exile with Zerubbabel.
Senaah.note: Location: Sometimes identified with Khirbat al Beiyudat.

definitions: Senate; a council or assembly of elders or rulers.
Plural form of 'senator'. See 'senator'.
Sendest; means thou sendest or dispatchest.
Sends; dispatches; causes to go forth.
Sending; present participial form of 'send', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'send'.
Seneh; one of the two crags beside the pass where Jonathan attacked the Philistine garrison.
Seneh.note: Location: At the pass near Michmash opposite Bozez, in the Benjamin hill country north of Jerusalem.

definitions: Senir; a name used for Hermon by other peoples.
Senir.note: Location: Usually placed at or identified with Mount Hermon.

definitions: Sennacherib; a man named in Scripture, father of Adrammelech.
Sense; meaning, understanding, perception, or faculty of feeling.
Plural form of 'sense'. See 'sense'.
governed by bodily appetite rather than the spirit.
Dispatched, commissioned, or caused to go.
a judgment, decree, or complete saying.
Plural form of 'sentence'. See 'sentence'.
Sentest; older second-person singular form of 'sent', used with 'thou'. See 'sent'.
Senuah; an ancestral or family name in the post-exilic records of Benjamin.
Seorim; the head of one of the priestly courses.
Separate; to divide, part, set apart, or keep distinct.
As an adjective, divided or distinct.
Separated; past tense or past participial form of 'separate'. See 'separate'.
Separateth; older third-person singular form of 'separate'. See 'separate'.
Separating; present participial form of 'separate', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'separate'.
the act or state of being set apart or divided.
Sephar; a place marking the eastern extent of Joktanite settlement in the table of nations.
Sephar.note: Location: In southern Arabia, often linked with the Yemen highlands.

definitions: Sepharad; the distant land named in Obadiah as a place of Jewish exile.
Sepharvaim; the Mesopotamian city whose people were resettled by Assyria in Samaria.
Sepharvaim.note: Location: Often identified with a site between Damascus and Hamath, though the exact identification is debated.

definitions: Sepharvites; people from Sepharvaim settled in Samaria by Assyrian resettlement.
A sepulchre; a tomb or burial place.
Plural form of 'sepulchre'. See 'sepulchre'.
Serah; the daughter of Asher, notably preserved in the tribal genealogies.
Seraiah; especially the high priest slain at Riblah, and also the name of various priests or officials in later records.
Plural form of 'seraphim'. See 'seraphim'.
Sered; a son of Zebulun and forefather of the Sardite family.
the Roman name borne by Sergius Paulus.
Plural form of 'serjeant'. See 'serjeant'.
A serpent; a snake, and figuratively a symbol of craft, danger, or Satanic evil.
Plural form of 'serpent'. See 'serpent'.
Serug; a patriarch in the line from Shem to Abraham.
One who serves another under authority.
The word may refer to a household servant, official minister, or bond-servant of God.
Servants; those who serve under authority, whether bondmen, attendants, or ministers.
Served; ministered, worked under authority, or rendered worship.
Servedst; means thou didst serve.
Servest; older second-person singular form of 'serve', used with 'thou'. See 'serve'.
Serves; ministers to; is in bondage or obedience to.
Service; labor done for another, ministry, or appointed duty.
Servile; slavish, meanly dependent, or belonging to a bond-service condition.
Serving; present participial form of 'serve', denoting ongoing action or process. See 'serve'.
Servitor; a servant or attendant.
slavery, bondage, or a state of service.
To value, esteem, or make account of.
Seth; older third-person singular form of 's'. See 's'.
Sethur; the son of Michael and spy of the tribe of Asher.
Setter; one who sets, places, or appoints.
Settest; thou settest; you set, place, appoint, or fix.
Sets; places; appoints; establishes.
Setting; the act of placing, appointing, fixing, or the state and context in which something is set.
Plural form of 'setting'. See 'setting'.
To establish, fix firmly, or bring into a stable state.
Settlest; older second-person singular form of 'settle', used with 'thou'. See 'settle'.
The number seven.
Sevenfold; seven times as much, or in a sevenfold manner.
Plural form of 'seven'. See 'seven'.
The number seventeen.
Next after the sixteenth.
The one next after the sixth; occupying the seventh place.
The number seventy.
Sever; to cut off, divide, or separate.
Separate; distinct; set apart from one another.
In a several manner or degree.
Severed; past tense or past participial form of 'sever'. See 'sever'.
Sharpness, strictness, or stern dealing.
Sew; to join or fasten with needle and thread.
Sewed; past tense or past participial form of 'sew'. See 'sew'.
Sewest; older second-person singular form of 'sew', used with 'thou'. See 'sew'.
Seweth; older third-person singular form of 'sew'. See 'sew'.
Shaalabbin; a town in the territory of Dan, also known in related form as Shaalbim.
Shaalbim; a Danite town from which the Amorites were not fully expelled, later incorporated into Solomon's administration.
Shaalbonite; an inhabitant, descendant, or member associated with Shaalbon in Scripture.
Shaaph; the name of men in Judahite genealogies, including one associated with the fatherhood of cities.
Shaaraim; a Judahite town in the lowland, notable in the route of the Philistines' flight after Goliath's fall.
Shaaraim.note: Location: Often identified with a site in the Ela Valley, though Tall al Ajjul is one proposal.

definitions: Shaashgaz; the chamberlain of Ahasuerus who kept the concubines.
Shabbethai; a Levite active in teaching and administration after the exile.
Shachia; a Benjamite name in the genealogies.
Shade; shadow, shelter from light, or a degree of difference.
As a verb, to cover with shade or shadow.
Shade or dark outline caused by intercepted light.
Figuratively, a dim image, shelter, or foreshadowing of something greater.
